title: Deep Learning meets Nonparametric Regression: Are Weight-Decayed DNNs Locally Adaptive?

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper examines training a specific neural network (namely parallel neural net) using weight decay and shows that the error rate matches the minimax bound for a specific class of functions (i.e., functions with bounded variation). 

It looks like a quite interesting effort and the result also appears to be significant. But I worry the paper is not presented at the level for general audience in ICLR. For example, I have a few questions/confusions after reading the submission: 

1. What exactly is weight decay? I actually could not find its definition/elaboration except for eq. 2, which is just L2 regularization? I was guessing it could relate to learning rate, momentum, some dynamic shrinkage on the fly...
2. Do we assume that Eq.2 can be optimally solved? It is a minimax result related to sample complexity so I believe the authors assume that Eq. can be optimally solved --- I think sometimes ignoring computational tractability is fine (and perhaps had been a standard practice in statistics) but it would be helpful to bring this upfront. 
3. What's so special about parallel NN? I think most audience wants to understand at a higher level where the magic happens and why the magic wont happen for the standard MLP, e.g., only limit of existing theoretical tools or parallel NN possesses some special structure? 
----
Thank you for addressing the presentation concern. I have raised my scores to 6 -- do not feel am an expert in this area so no confidence to move to 7.

Weakness: there is room for improvement in presentation.

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper studies the statistical performance of a weight‐decayed parallel neural network in nonparametric regression on bounded‐variation (BV) function classes. The main theoretical contribution is to show that, under weight decay, a parallel architecture attains the minimax error rate for BV function classes. The analysis leverages covering‐number arguments tailored to the parallel network structure to derive sample‐complexity guarantees. This is an interesting and nontrivial result, but several presentation issues limit its accessibility to the broader ICLR audience.

Strengths  
1. Novel statistical guarantee: The paper rigorously proves that training a parallel neural network with an ℓ2 weight‐decay penalty achieves the minimax optimal rate for BV‐type function spaces.  
2. Technical sophistication: The derivation employs nontrivial covering‐number techniques adapted to the parallel architecture, demonstrating a deep grasp of statistical learning theory.

Weaknesses  
1. Unclear presentation of weight decay: The paper only introduces “weight decay” via Eq. (2) as an ℓ2 penalty, without explaining its algorithmic or practical interpretation.  
2. Hidden computational assumptions: It appears the authors assume the regularized objective (Eq. (2)) is solved to global optimality, but this assumption is never explicitly stated or justified in terms of tractability.  
3. Role of parallel architecture underexplained: The manuscript does not conceptually clarify why the parallel network structure is crucial for the analysis and why a standard multilayer perceptron (MLP) would not satisfy the same covering‐number bounds.  
4. Exposition gaps: Key concepts and structural assumptions are scattered or deferred, leaving readers confused about the setting and basic definitions before the technical sections.

Detailed Comments and Suggestions  
1. Definition and discussion of weight decay  
   – Beyond Eq. (2), please provide an explicit definition of weight decay and discuss how it is implemented in practice (e.g., continuous‐time interpretation, equivalence to a penalty on weight dynamics).  
2. Explicit computational assumptions  
   – Clearly state that your analysis assumes the regularized training objective can be optimized to global optimality. Discuss the plausibility of this assumption or its impact if only approximate solutions are available.  
3. Clarify the parallel network advantage  
   – Offer a high‐level explanation of the structural property of parallel networks (e.g., layerwise decomposition, parameter sharing) that enables tighter covering‐number bounds compared to an MLP. This will help readers appreciate why the result does not immediately extend to standard architectures.  
4. Improve exposition and notation  
   – Introduce all key concepts (weight decay, BV function classes, computational assumptions) in the preliminaries.  
   – Provide a guided roadmap at the start of Section 3 to orient the reader before delving into technical lemmas.  
   – Consider illustrative examples or diagrams to convey the intuition behind your main proof steps.

Main Points to Address  
– The current reliance on Eq. (2) as the sole definition of weight decay leaves readers without a clear understanding of its algorithmic role.  
– The hidden global‐optimality assumption for the penalized objective should be made explicit and its implications discussed.  
– A conceptual discussion is needed on why parallel NNs admit this minimax guarantee while standard MLPs do not.  
– Several definitions and assumptions are introduced late in the manuscript, causing confusion; reorganizing the preliminaries will improve clarity.

Recommendation  
This work makes a valuable contribution by establishing a minimax‐rate result for parallel neural networks with weight decay and employs solid theoretical techniques. However, to make the paper accessible to ICLR’s broad audience, the authors should substantially improve the clarity of definitions, explicitly state their computational assumptions, and provide more intuition on the role of parallel architectures. I recommend marginal acceptance (6: marginally above the acceptance threshold), contingent upon thorough revisions to address the presentation and exposition gaps outlined above.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper analyzes parallel neural networks for nonparametric regression (including approximation error and regression error analysis). Suppose the regression function has nonhomogeneous regularities, e.g., Besov functions or functions of bounded variation. Parallel neural networks trained with weight decay achieves near optimal estimation, indicating the adaptability of parallel neural networks.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
============= Strength =============

The paper is clearly written with both theoretical and empirical results. The theoretical results are correct and empirical results are supportive.

The adaptability of neural networks is studied in many recent works, including its adaptability to data geometry and adaptability to structures in function spaces. This paper considers parallel neural networks and uses weight decay for controlling the complexity of the network, which circumvents sparsity constraints in many existing network approximation results.

============= Weakness =============

There is no related work section. Some of papers share similar ideas to parallel neural networks, for example, "Approximation and Non-parametric Estimation of ResNet-type Convolutional Neural Networks" and "Besov Function Approximation and Binary Classification on Low-Dimensional Manifolds Using Convolutional Residual Networks" both rely on implementing block sparse feedforward networks. The resulting (residual) convolutional network does not need a sparsity constraint in training.

The obtained rate of estimation is slightly slower than the optimal rate, which is attainable (up to log factors) in many recent works on using ReLU neural networks for nonparametric regression.

From experiments, parallel neural networks seem to achieve comparable performance to neural networks and conventional methods.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Some of the technical contributions need highlighting. For example, the conversion of weight decay objective to the constraint form in Equation (5) can be highlighted as a contribution -- if it does not appear in Parhi & Nowak (2021a). Another example is the decomposition of \mathcal{F}_{\parallel} and \mathcal{F}_{\perp}.

A discussion on why parallel neural networks can only achieve the optimal rate in the asymptotic sense ($L \to \infty$) is helpful. Is it because the weight decay introduces additional bias?

In Theorem 1, it is good to state how to choose weight decay $\lambda$.

Lemma 6 is relative difficult to understand, mainly because the first sentence begins with an equation.

Overall, the paper is well written. There are some typos and typeset issues, for example, "errorand" --> "error and" above Section 4.2
reviews.summary_of_the_review: I think the paper is interesting. I am currently giving a slightly negative rating, due to some concerns of the results and missing references. I am happy to raise to a positive rating after paper revision and an effective author feedback.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper analyzes parallel neural networks for nonparametric regression (including approximation error and regression error analysis). Suppose the regression function has nonhomogeneous regularities, e.g., Besov functions or functions of bounded variation. Parallel neural networks trained with weight decay achieves near optimal estimation, indicating the adaptability of parallel neural networks.

Strength And Weaknesses:
============= Strength =============

The paper is clearly written with both theoretical and empirical results. The theoretical results are correct and empirical results are supportive.

The adaptability of neural networks is studied in many recent works, including its adaptability to data geometry and adaptability to structures in function spaces. This paper considers parallel neural networks and uses weight decay for controlling the complexity of the network, which circumvents sparsity constraints in many existing network approximation results.

============= Weakness =============

There is no related work section. Some of papers share similar ideas to parallel neural networks, for example, "Approximation and Non-parametric Estimation of ResNet-type Convolutional Neural Networks" and "Besov Function Approximation and Binary Classification on Low-Dimensional Manifolds Using Convolutional Residual Networks" both rely on implementing block sparse feedforward networks. The resulting (residual) convolutional network does not need a sparsity constraint in training.

The obtained rate of estimation is slightly slower than the optimal rate, which is attainable (up to log factors) in many recent works on using ReLU neural networks for nonparametric regression.

From experiments, parallel neural networks seem to achieve comparable performance to neural networks and conventional methods.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Some of the technical contributions need highlighting. For example, the conversion of weight decay objective to the constraint form in Equation (5) can be highlighted as a contribution -- if it does not appear in Parhi & Nowak (2021a). Another example is the decomposition of \mathcal{F}_{\parallel} and \mathcal{F}_{\perp}.

A discussion on why parallel neural networks can only achieve the optimal rate in the asymptotic sense ($L \to \infty$) is helpful. Is it because the weight decay introduces additional bias?

In Theorem 1, it is good to state how to choose weight decay $\lambda$.

Lemma 6 is relative difficult to understand, mainly because the first sentence begins with an equation.

Overall, the paper is well written. There are some typos and typeset issues, for example, "errorand" --> "error and" above Section 4.2

Summary Of The Review:
I think the paper is interesting. I am currently giving a slightly negative rating, due to some concerns of the results and missing references. I am happy to raise to a positive rating after paper revision and an effective author feedback.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper studies the estimation properties of parallel neural networks with ℓ₂ weight decay for nonparametric regression under heterogeneous smoothness. The authors present a clear theoretical development demonstrating that such architectures adapt nearly optimally (up to logarithmic factors) to unknown smoothness levels, and they complement their analysis with empirical experiments that validate the theoretical findings. Overall, I find the manuscript well written and technically sound, and I recommend acceptance (rating 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold), subject to revisions addressing the points below.

Strengths  
1. Clarity and Exposition  
   – The paper is very well organized, with precise definitions and clean notation. Both the theory and experiments are presented in a way that is easy to follow.  
2. Sound Theoretical Analysis  
   – The main result (Theorem 1) correctly characterizes the nearly minimax‐optimal estimation error for functions of heterogeneous smoothness, and the proof techniques appear rigorous.  
3. Supportive Empirical Validation  
   – Numerical experiments confirm that parallel networks with weight decay adapt to varying smoothness levels of target functions, demonstrating the practical relevance of the theory.  
4. Novel Perspective on Adaptivity  
   – By focusing on parallel architectures and leveraging ℓ₂ regularization rather than sparsity constraints, the work highlights an interesting mechanism by which neural networks can self‐tune to unknown smoothness.

Weaknesses and Points for Revision  
1. Missing Related Work Section  
   – The manuscript currently lacks a dedicated discussion of prior results on related architectures. In particular, it should cite and contrast with “Approximation and Non‐parametric Estimation of ResNet‐type CNNs” and “Besov Function Approximation and Binary Classification on Low‐Dimensional Manifolds Using Convolutional Residual Networks.”  
2. Suboptimal Convergence Rates  
   – While the derived error exponent matches known lower bounds up to logarithmic factors, it remains slightly slower than the optimal ReLU‐NN rates reported in the recent literature. The manuscript would benefit from a short discussion of whether this gap is inherent to the parallel‐network construction or an artifact of the analysis.  
3. Empirical Comparison  
   – In experiments the parallel networks perform only on par with standard (nonparallel) models. It would strengthen the paper to highlight scenarios where the parallel design gives a clear advantage, or at least to discuss why superiority was not observed in the current simulations.  
4. Underemphasized Technical Contributions  
   – Two key ideas—the conversion of the weight‐decay objective into a constrained ℓ_{2/L} penalty (Equation 5) and the decomposition of the function class into the subspaces F_{∥} and F_{⊥}—are buried in the technical sections. These deserve to be highlighted more prominently in the introduction or a dedicated “Contributions” subsection.  
5. Asymptotic Depth Requirement  
   – The optimal rates are only guaranteed asymptotically as network depth L→∞. The paper does not discuss how finite L introduces extra bias via weight decay. A brief explanation of this phenomenon would clarify the practical implications.  
6. Regularization Parameter Selection  
   – Theorem 1 establishes risk bounds in terms of the regularization weight λ, yet offers no guidance on how λ should be chosen in practice. Including an explicit recommendation or heuristic would be very helpful for practitioners.  
7. Clarity of Lemma 6  
   – Lemma 6 currently begins with an equation, making it hard to parse the lemma’s purpose and assumptions. I suggest rephrasing the opening paragraph to state the result in words before presenting the technical bound.  
8. Minor Typos and Typesetting  
   – A few small errors (e.g., “errorand” instead of “error and”) and inconsistent formatting should be corrected in a revision for polish.

Detailed Suggestions  
– Add a “Related Work” section that situates this study among recent results on block‐sparse feedforward networks and residual CNN approximation.  
– In the introduction or a dedicated subsection, call out as primary contributions: (i) the equivalence between ℓ₂ weight decay and a constrained ℓ_{2/L} norm, and (ii) the F_{∥}/F_{⊥} decomposition used in the proof of adaptivity.  
– Include a paragraph discussing potential sources of extra bias due to weight decay at finite depth and why only asymptotic L→∞ guarantees exact optimality.  
– Extend Theorem 1 (or its discussion) to offer practical advice on tuning λ, perhaps based on sample size n, network width, and depth.  
– Improve the exposition of Lemma 6 by introducing it in prose before giving the formal covering‐number bound.  
– Perform a pass to fix typos and unify notation (for example, ensure consistent spacing around mathematical operators).

Conclusion  
This paper makes a solid contribution to our understanding of how parallel neural networks with ℓ₂ regularization can adapt to heterogeneous smoothness in a nonparametric regression setting. The theoretical results are correct and interesting, and the experiments support the main claims. At the same time, the manuscript would be significantly strengthened by adding a related work section, better highlighting its technical innovations, clarifying several theoretical points, and fixing minor presentation issues. I recommend acceptance, contingent on these revisions.

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper analyzes parallel neural networks for nonparametric regression (including approximation error and regression error analysis). Suppose the regression function has nonhomogeneous regularities, e.g., Besov functions or functions of bounded variation. Parallel neural networks trained with weight decay achieves near optimal estimation, indicating the adaptability of parallel neural networks.

Strength And Weaknesses:
============= Strength =============

The paper is clearly written with both theoretical and empirical results. The theoretical results are correct and empirical results are supportive.

The adaptability of neural networks is studied in many recent works, including its adaptability to data geometry and adaptability to structures in function spaces. This paper considers parallel neural networks and uses weight decay for controlling the complexity of the network, which circumvents sparsity constraints in many existing network approximation results.

============= Weakness =============

There is no related work section. Some of papers share similar ideas to parallel neural networks, for example, "Approximation and Non-parametric Estimation of ResNet-type Convolutional Neural Networks" and "Besov Function Approximation and Binary Classification on Low-Dimensional Manifolds Using Convolutional Residual Networks" both rely on implementing block sparse feedforward networks. The resulting (residual) convolutional network does not need a sparsity constraint in training.

The obtained rate of estimation is slightly slower than the optimal rate, which is attainable (up to log factors) in many recent works on using ReLU neural networks for nonparametric regression.

From experiments, parallel neural networks seem to achieve comparable performance to neural networks and conventional methods.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Some of the technical contributions need highlighting. For example, the conversion of weight decay objective to the constraint form in Equation (5) can be highlighted as a contribution -- if it does not appear in Parhi & Nowak (2021a). Another example is the decomposition of \mathcal{F}_{\parallel} and \mathcal{F}_{\perp}.

A discussion on why parallel neural networks can only achieve the optimal rate in the asymptotic sense ($L \to \infty$) is helpful. Is it because the weight decay introduces additional bias?

In Theorem 1, it is good to state how to choose weight decay $\lambda$.

Lemma 6 is relative difficult to understand, mainly because the first sentence begins with an equation.

Overall, the paper is well written. There are some typos and typeset issues, for example, "errorand" --> "error and" above Section 4.2

Summary Of The Review:
I think the paper is interesting. I am currently giving a slightly negative rating, due to some concerns of the results and missing references. I am happy to raise to a positive rating after paper revision and an effective author feedback.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes to analyze the theory of deep neural network from the perspective of non-parametric regression. The authors show that training an $L$ layer neural network with weight decay is basically the same as penalized regression. And in the case of functions in the Besov space and bounded variation space, neural networks achieves almost near-optimal rates. When the number of layers increase, the error decreases and thus explains why deep networks performs better than shallow ones. The authors also validate some of their claims using experiments.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:
  1. The paper is well-written and mostly clear.
  2. The theoretical analysis provided by the authors are, as far as I am aware of, novel and interesting.
  3. The obtained results do provide some insights on, for example, why deep networks do no overfit and why deep networks perform better than shallow networks.

Weaknesses:
  
 First off, I want to say that I am very new to the area of non-parametric regression and the function spaces the authors use in this paper. I have some experience with deep learning theory but this direction is completely new to me. Therefore, I might not be the best reviewer for this paper. Some of my comments might be unfair/wrong and I hope the authors and the other reviewers can help me understand this paper a little more during the discussion period. 

  Now back to this paper, I do have some questions about the setting and the results the authors provide. 
  
  1. The authors have chosen to optimize a set of parallel feedforward networks with $L_2$ regularization (I don't really like using the word "weight decay" here because there is no mentioning of the optimizer, e.g. SGD/Adam, and thus weight decay does not really exist). I wonder why the authors didn't include the regularization for the bias in Eqn. (2)? After looking at the Appendix, I think the authors do analyze the network with the bias term, so why is the regularization for bias missing? I think in practice, people have both regularization for weights and bias.

  2. In Theorem 1, I am confused by the statement "with proper choice of the parameter of weight decay $\lambda$". How large does the parameter need to be? Does it need to be time-varying, for example? If the choice of $\lambda$ needs to depend on the other parameters such as $M, L, p, q$, please state it explicitly in the theorems so that we can understand it more clearly.
  
  3. I am a little confused by the results in Corollary 2 and 3. For example, in Cor 2, although the $o(1)$ term is negligible, does it mean that the rate of deep neural network is worse than that of traditional nonparametric regression models? Are there models that can achieve the minimax rates that the authors have state Page 3? I think it might be better to have a table to compare the results in this paper with the results in the literature. Also, what is going to happen if $L > 100 C \log n$? For very deep neural networks, do the theorems still hold?

  4. For Cor 3, although I know that most of the paper is focused on $L\gg 2$, the authors might want to mention that the results here fail for $L=1, 2$. Also, the claim that "DNN has an advantage over kernels" is a little confusing, shouldn't there be an assumption on $L$ (L> some function of m) so that the bound for DNN is strictly better than that of kernels? I hope the authors can state it more clearly.

  I do not have enough time to go through all the proof details, but the proof overview section does give me enough intuition of how the theorems are derived. The experimental results are a good supplement to the theory.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: The paper is mostly clear, novel, and reproducible with the appendix.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, this seems to be a good paper with nice contributions. However, I am confused by some settings and theoretical results of the authors. Again, I am no expert in this field, and if there is anything wrong in my review, please correct me and I am happy to increase my score.
reviews.confidence: 2: You are willing to defend your assessment, but it is quite likely that you did not understand the central parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes to analyze the theory of deep neural network from the perspective of non-parametric regression. The authors show that training an $L$ layer neural network with weight decay is basically the same as penalized regression. And in the case of functions in the Besov space and bounded variation space, neural networks achieves almost near-optimal rates. When the number of layers increase, the error decreases and thus explains why deep networks performs better than shallow ones. The authors also validate some of their claims using experiments.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength:
  1. The paper is well-written and mostly clear.
  2. The theoretical analysis provided by the authors are, as far as I am aware of, novel and interesting.
  3. The obtained results do provide some insights on, for example, why deep networks do no overfit and why deep networks perform better than shallow networks.

Weaknesses:
  
 First off, I want to say that I am very new to the area of non-parametric regression and the function spaces the authors use in this paper. I have some experience with deep learning theory but this direction is completely new to me. Therefore, I might not be the best reviewer for this paper. Some of my comments might be unfair/wrong and I hope the authors and the other reviewers can help me understand this paper a little more during the discussion period. 

  Now back to this paper, I do have some questions about the setting and the results the authors provide. 
  
  1. The authors have chosen to optimize a set of parallel feedforward networks with $L_2$ regularization (I don't really like using the word "weight decay" here because there is no mentioning of the optimizer, e.g. SGD/Adam, and thus weight decay does not really exist). I wonder why the authors didn't include the regularization for the bias in Eqn. (2)? After looking at the Appendix, I think the authors do analyze the network with the bias term, so why is the regularization for bias missing? I think in practice, people have both regularization for weights and bias.

  2. In Theorem 1, I am confused by the statement "with proper choice of the parameter of weight decay $\lambda$". How large does the parameter need to be? Does it need to be time-varying, for example? If the choice of $\lambda$ needs to depend on the other parameters such as $M, L, p, q$, please state it explicitly in the theorems so that we can understand it more clearly.
  
  3. I am a little confused by the results in Corollary 2 and 3. For example, in Cor 2, although the $o(1)$ term is negligible, does it mean that the rate of deep neural network is worse than that of traditional nonparametric regression models? Are there models that can achieve the minimax rates that the authors have state Page 3? I think it might be better to have a table to compare the results in this paper with the results in the literature. Also, what is going to happen if $L > 100 C \log n$? For very deep neural networks, do the theorems still hold?

  4. For Cor 3, although I know that most of the paper is focused on $L\gg 2$, the authors might want to mention that the results here fail for $L=1, 2$. Also, the claim that "DNN has an advantage over kernels" is a little confusing, shouldn't there be an assumption on $L$ (L> some function of m) so that the bound for DNN is strictly better than that of kernels? I hope the authors can state it more clearly.

  I do not have enough time to go through all the proof details, but the proof overview section does give me enough intuition of how the theorems are derived. The experimental results are a good supplement to the theory.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The paper is mostly clear, novel, and reproducible with the appendix.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, this seems to be a good paper with nice contributions. However, I am confused by some settings and theoretical results of the authors. Again, I am no expert in this field, and if there is anything wrong in my review, please correct me and I am happy to increase my score.

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper investigates the implicit regularization induced by weight decay in deep ReLU networks, showing that training with ℓ2 weight decay corresponds to an ℓp‐penalized nonparametric regression estimator. The authors prove that such networks can attain near‐minimax estimation rates over Besov(α,p,q) and BV(m) function spaces, offer insights into why deep architectures avoid overfitting compared to shallow ones, and support their theory with experiments demonstrating local adaptivity.

Strengths:
1. The manuscript is well written and the presentation is largely clear, making sophisticated arguments accessible.
2. The theoretical connection between weight decay and adaptive nonparametric regression is novel and deepens our understanding of why deep networks can automatically adapt to function smoothness.
3. The work offers concrete insights into how network depth and weight‐decay jointly govern model complexity, which helps explain the empirical success of deep versus shallow architectures.
4. The experimental section effectively illustrates the local adaptivity properties predicted by the theory.

Weaknesses:
1. In Equation (2), only the weights are penalized; no regularization term for biases is specified, although in practice biases often receive weight‐decay treatment as well.
2. Theorem 1’s statement that the estimator achieves the claimed rate “with proper choice of λ” is vague—there is no explicit guidance on how λ should scale with network width M, depth L, smoothness parameters p, q, or sample size n.
3. Corollary 2 incurs an o(1) factor in the exponent of the convergence rate O(n^{−2α/(2α+d)(1−o(1))}), suggesting that deep nets may suffer a worse leading‐order rate than classical minimax procedures. The paper does not clearly position this result relative to known minimax‐optimal estimators.
4. In Corollary 3, the claimed strict advantage over kernel methods via BV(m) approximation lacks a precise condition on the network depth L (for example, a lower bound on L). Edge cases, such as L=1 or L=2, are not discussed, nor is the behavior when L ≫ log n.
5. There is no explicit comparison or tabulation of the paper’s rates alongside classical minimax rates from the literature (as mentioned on Page 3), making it difficult to assess practical gains.

Suggestions for Improvement:
1. Update Equation (2) to include a bias regularization term, or explicitly justify why biases are left unpenalized in your theoretical framework.
2. In Theorem 1, provide an explicit formula or asymptotic scaling for λ as a function of M, L, p, q, and n. This will help practitioners choose λ in real‐world settings.
3. Introduce a comparison table that summarizes the minimax rates from existing works (e.g., wavelet estimators, kernel methods) alongside your deep network rates to make the relative performance clear.
4. Clarify the valid range of the network depth L for which Corollary 3’s claimed advantage over kernels holds. In particular, specify a lower bound on L in terms of m and discuss what happens when L is very small (L=1,2) or very large (L≫log n).
5. Expand the discussion around the o(1) factor in Corollary 2: is it an artifact of the proof or an inherent limitation? If possible, tighten the analysis or discuss scenarios in which the o(1) term becomes negligible.

Conclusion:
Overall, this paper offers a significant and novel theoretical contribution linking weight decay to adaptive nonparametric estimation, backed by illustrative experiments. The main concerns relate to certain modeling choices (bias regularization), the clarity of parameter scaling (choice of λ), and the need for more explicit comparisons to classical minimax results. Addressing these points would considerably strengthen the manuscript. I recommend acceptance, contingent on minor-to-moderate revisions.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes to analyze the theory of deep neural network from the perspective of non-parametric regression. The authors show that training an $L$ layer neural network with weight decay is basically the same as penalized regression. And in the case of functions in the Besov space and bounded variation space, neural networks achieves almost near-optimal rates. When the number of layers increase, the error decreases and thus explains why deep networks performs better than shallow ones. The authors also validate some of their claims using experiments.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength:

  2. The theoretical analysis provided by the authors are, as far as I am aware of, novel and interesting.

Weaknesses:
  
 First off, I want to say that I am very new to the area of non-parametric regression and the function spaces the authors use in this paper. I have some experience with deep learning theory but this direction is completely new to me. Therefore, I might not be the best reviewer for this paper. Some of my comments might be unfair/wrong and I hope the authors and the other reviewers can help me understand this paper a little more during the discussion period. 

  Now back to this paper, I do have some questions about the setting and the results the authors provide. 
  
  1. The authors have chosen to optimize a set of parallel feedforward networks with $L_2$ regularization (I don't really like using the word "weight decay" here because there is no mentioning of the optimizer, e.g. SGD/Adam, and thus weight decay does not really exist). I wonder why the authors didn't include the regularization for the bias in Eqn. (2)? I think in practice, people have both regularization for weights and bias.

  2. In Theorem 1, I am confused by the statement "with proper choice of the parameter of weight decay $\lambda$". How large does the parameter need to be? Does it need to be time-varying, for example? If the choice of $\lambda$ needs to depend on the other parameters such as $M, L, p, q$, please state it explicitly in the theorems so that we can understand it more clearly.
  
  3. I am a little confused by the results in Corollary 2 and 3. For example, in Cor 2, although the $o(1)$ term is negligible, does it mean that the rate of deep neural network is worse than that of traditional nonparametric regression models? Are there models that can achieve the minimax rates that the authors have state Page 3? I think it might be better to have a table to compare the results in this paper with the results in the literature. Also, what is going to happen if $L > 100 C \log n$? For very deep neural networks, do the theorems still hold?

  4. For Cor 3, although I know that most of the paper is focused on $L\gg 2$, the authors might want to mention that the results here fail for $L=1, 2$. Also, the claim that "DNN has an advantage over kernels" is a little confusing, shouldn't there be an assumption on $L$ (L> some function of m) so that the bound for DNN is strictly better than that of kernels? I hope the authors can state it more clearly.

  I do not have enough time to go through all the proof details, but the proof overview section does give me enough intuition of how the theorems are derived. The experimental results are a good supplement to the theory.

The paper is mostly clear, novel, and reproducible with the appendix.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, this seems to be a good paper with nice contributions. However, I am confused by some settings and theoretical results of the authors. Again, I am no expert in this field, and if there is anything wrong in my review, please correct me and I am happy to increase my score.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper extends Suzuki’s work (ICLR2018) to parallel neural networks in the Besov space as well as BV functions, studies the generalization properties in terms of approximation error via B-spline basis and estimation error via covering number, and demonstrates the $\ell_2$ norm (weight decay) in parallel ReLU neural networks is equivalent to a certain sparse regularization term.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
**Pros:** 

1. This work allows for over-parameterization without the extra sparsity condition
The obtained bound of the covering number is independent of the number of subnetworks, which is substituted by the Frobenius norm.  

**Cons:**
 
1. The motivation on locally adaptivity makes sense, comparing linear estimators and DNNs. Nevertheless, the motivation on the comparison of (Suzuki ICLR2019)  is relatively weak. The authors claim that Suzuki’s work requires certain conditions on the width, the depth, the sparsity to achieve the minimax rate on deep ReLU networks, which is different from practical uses. Nevertheless, this work does not focus on this issue but centers around another architecture, i.e., parallel neural networks. Even though parallel neural networks have theoretical and empirical applications as the authors suggested, the “correct” motivation is to solve certain conditions in Suzuki’s work under the same architecture.

More importantly, this work also requires several conditions on the width, the depth, etc. In the “No architecture tuning” part, the required architecture of the model still depends on $\alpha$, see Theorem 1. It is hard for me to accept the authors’ motivation.


2. In theorem 1, the number of subnetworks is exponentially large enough w.r.t the input dimension $d$. This is unacceptable in both theory and practice.


3. In Proposition 4, the equivalence between Eq. (2) and Eq. (12) is imprecise via the Lagrangian’s method in optimization. The constraint value P doesn't appear in Eq. (2) and it is also not clear how the dual variable is handled. In fact P (as well as P’) is unknown or uncomputable, which also makes the upper bound of the covering number appear infeasible in Theorem 5 depending on P’. It is ok for ease of description but is not rigorous in a theory paper corresponding to the first contribution.

4. After reading this paper, I think the technical contribution is to derive the covering number bound of the parallel neural networks for the estimation error, i.e., Theorem 5 and Lemma 6. The proof for approximation error follows Yarotsky (2017); Suzuki (2018). However, this paper works under a fixed design, which is much easier to derive the estimation error.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
This paper is well-written. Apart from the above mentioned issues, I have other questions that

1. I agree with the authors that kernel methods, e.g., NTK, lacks local adaptivity to some extent due to RKHS. I’m wondering that, the mean field regime (instead of NTK) is also suboptimal to local adaptivity?


2. For the random design, the proof of estimation error can be similarly established by [1]?

[1] Schmidt-Hieber, Johannes. "Nonparametric regression using deep neural networks with ReLU activation function." The Annals of Statistics 48, no. 4 (2020): 1875-1897.

Nevertheless, the derived results appear vacuous in terms of imprecise Lagrangian dual and curse of dimensionality of the input dimension.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper extends Suzuki’s work (ICLR2018) to parallel neural networks in the Besov space as well as BV functions, studies the generalization properties in terms of approximation error via B-spline basis and estimation error via covering number, and demonstrates the $\ell_2$ norm (weight decay) in parallel ReLU neural networks is equivalent to a certain sparse regularization term.

Strength And Weaknesses:
**Pros:** 

1. This work allows for over-parameterization without the extra sparsity condition
The obtained bound of the covering number is independent of the number of subnetworks, which is substituted by the Frobenius norm.  

**Cons:**
 
1. The motivation on locally adaptivity makes sense, comparing linear estimators and DNNs. Nevertheless, the motivation on the comparison of (Suzuki ICLR2019)  is relatively weak. The authors claim that Suzuki’s work requires certain conditions on the width, the depth, the sparsity to achieve the minimax rate on deep ReLU networks, which is different from practical uses. Nevertheless, this work does not focus on this issue but centers around another architecture, i.e., parallel neural networks. Even though parallel neural networks have theoretical and empirical applications as the authors suggested, the “correct” motivation is to solve certain conditions in Suzuki’s work under the same architecture.

More importantly, this work also requires several conditions on the width, the depth, etc. In the “No architecture tuning” part, the required architecture of the model still depends on $\alpha$, see Theorem 1. It is hard for me to accept the authors’ motivation.


2. In theorem 1, the number of subnetworks is exponentially large enough w.r.t the input dimension $d$. This is unacceptable in both theory and practice.


3. In Proposition 4, the equivalence between Eq. (2) and Eq. (12) is imprecise via the Lagrangian’s method in optimization. The constraint value P doesn't appear in Eq. (2) and it is also not clear how the dual variable is handled. In fact P (as well as P’) is unknown or uncomputable, which also makes the upper bound of the covering number appear infeasible in Theorem 5 depending on P’. It is ok for ease of description but is not rigorous in a theory paper corresponding to the first contribution.

4. After reading this paper, I think the technical contribution is to derive the covering number bound of the parallel neural networks for the estimation error, i.e., Theorem 5 and Lemma 6. The proof for approximation error follows Yarotsky (2017); Suzuki (2018). However, this paper works under a fixed design, which is much easier to derive the estimation error.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
This paper is well-written. Apart from the above mentioned issues, I have other questions that

1. I agree with the authors that kernel methods, e.g., NTK, lacks local adaptivity to some extent due to RKHS. I’m wondering that, the mean field regime (instead of NTK) is also suboptimal to local adaptivity?


2. For the random design, the proof of estimation error can be similarly established by [1]?

[1] Schmidt-Hieber, Johannes. "Nonparametric regression using deep neural networks with ReLU activation function." The Annals of Statistics 48, no. 4 (2020): 1875-1897.

Summary Of The Review:
This work has some good results on the sparsity, the separation between linear estimators and DNNs in Besov spaces as well as BV functions. Nevertheless, the derived results appear vacuous in terms of imprecise Lagrangian dual and curse of dimensionality of the input dimension.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This manuscript investigates adaptive estimation in Besov and bounded-variation function classes by extending the line of work initiated by Suzuki (ICLR 2018/2019). In particular, the authors propose using parallel ReLU networks with ℓ₂ weight decay and show that this regularization implicitly induces an ℓₚ (p=2/L) penalty on the basis coefficients. They further derive a covering‐number bound for the resulting function class that depends on the total Frobenius norm of the network weights rather than on the number of subnetworks. Under fixed‐design assumptions, the paper combines these approximation and estimation bounds to claim minimax‐optimal rates without explicit sparsity constraints on individual subnetworks.

Strengths  
1. Over-parameterization without extra sparsity constraints  
   The approach allows the network to be heavily over‐parameterized—i.e., with many subnetworks—while relying solely on a global Frobenius‐norm constraint instead of imposing hard sparsity on each subnetwork.  
2. Covering‐number bound independent of subnetwork count  
   Theorem 5 replaces the usual dependence on the number of subnetworks M with a bound controlled by the total Frobenius norm of the weight matrices, thus avoiding a direct M-dependence in the complexity measure.

Weaknesses  
1. Weak motivation relative to Suzuki’s setup  
   Although the authors aim to “improve” upon Suzuki’s adaptive estimator, the paper does so by changing the network architecture—introducing parallel subnetworks—rather than by relaxing or removing Suzuki’s width/depth/sparsity assumptions within the same architectural framework. This shift in model makes the motivation less compelling.  
2. Undermined “no architecture tuning” claim  
   Despite the rhetoric of not tuning architecture, Theorem 1 still requires the width, depth, and number of subnetworks to scale with the smoothness parameter α. Thus, practitioners must choose these hyperparameters based on α, contrary to the stated goal.  
3. Exponential subnetwork growth in dimension  
   When d is large, the number of required subnetworks M in Theorem 1 grows exponentially in d under the given smoothness regime, rendering the construction impractical.  
4. Imprecise variational equivalence (Proposition 4)  
   The derivation of the equivalence between the ℓ₂‐penalized network objective and an ℓₚ penalty on basis coefficients is not fully detailed. In particular, the constraint level P (and its dual counterpart P′) does not explicitly appear in Eq. (2), and the handling of Lagrange multipliers is unclear. As a result, the covering‐number bound in Theorem 5 depends on an unspecified P′, raising questions about its feasibility.  
5. Limited technical novelty  
   The approximation error analysis follows the B‐spline constructions of Yarotsky (2017) and Suzuki (2018), and the estimation‐error bound is derived under a fixed‐design assumption, which is simpler than the more challenging random-design setting.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Refine the motivation  
   Recast the paper’s goals to address Suzuki’s width/depth/sparsity constraints within the same single‐network setup, rather than introducing parallel subnetworks as a fundamentally new model. Demonstrating how ℓ₂ regularization alone could weaken Suzuki’s architectural assumptions would make the contribution more directly comparable.  
2. Clarify Proposition 4  
   Provide a step-by-step variational derivation that explicitly shows how the norm constraint P (and the resulting P′) arises from the Lagrangian dual, and how the regularization parameter λ maps to P. This will solidify the technical link between the ℓ₂ objective and the ℓₚ penalty.  
3. Discuss alternative training regimes  
   Assess whether the mean-field (infinite-width) training regime, as opposed to the NTK regime, can achieve or fail to achieve local adaptivity. Insights here could broaden the impact of the theoretical findings.  
4. Extend estimation to random design  
   To strengthen the generality of the results, adapt the estimation‐error proof to the random-design setting, potentially leveraging techniques from Schmidt-Hieber (2020) or related work.

Conclusion  
This paper tackles an interesting extension of adaptive estimation theory to parallel neural networks with weight decay, and it offers a novel perspective on complexity control via Frobenius‐norm bounds. However, the motivational framing, architectural dependencies, and precision of key theoretical arguments need significant refinement. Given these concerns and the limited advance over existing approximation techniques, I recommend a score of 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold.

extracted_ideas.summary: The reviewer finds the extension of Suzuki’s work to parallel neural networks and the equivalence between ℓ₂ regularization and a sparse penalty interesting, but questions the paper’s motivation, architectural requirements, and rigor of certain theoretical arguments.
strengths: Allows over-parameterization without imposing extra sparsity constraints.
Derives a covering‐number bound that is independent of the number of subnetworks, replacing that dependence with a Frobenius‐norm constraint.
weaknesses: Motivation for improving on Suzuki (ICLR 2018/2019) is weak, since the paper changes the architecture rather than undercutting Suzuki’s assumptions in the same setup.
The required network architecture (width, depth) still depends on the smoothness parameter α (Theorem 1), undermining the “no architecture tuning” claim.
Theorem 1 demands an exponentially large number of subnetworks in the input dimension d, which is impractical.

Proposition 4’s claimed equivalence via Lagrangian dual is imprecise: the constraint P does not appear in Eq. (2) and it is unclear how dual variables are handled, making P and P′ both unknown and the resulting covering‐number bound (Theorem 5) infeasible.
The technical novelty is limited: approximation results follow existing work (Yarotsky 2017; Suzuki 2018), and estimation error is derived under the easier fixed‐design setting.
suggestions: Frame the motivation so as to address Suzuki’s width/depth/sparsity conditions within the same architecture rather than introducing parallel networks as a new model.
Provide a precise derivation of the equivalence in Proposition 4, explicitly showing how the norm constraint P (and P′) emerges and how dual variables are handled.
Discuss whether the mean‐field training regime (rather than NTK) also fails to achieve local adaptivity.
For the random‐design setting, adapt the estimation‐error proof using techniques from Schmidt-Hieber (2020) to strengthen generality.
main_points: Paper extends Suzuki’s results on adaptive estimation in Besov/BV spaces to parallel ReLU networks with ℓ₂ regularization.
Shows ℓ₂ weight decay in parallel nets induces an ℓₚ (p=2/L) sparse penalty on learned basis coefficients.
Claims covering‐number bound independent of the number of subnetworks, controlled by Frobenius norms.
Still requires network hyperparameters (width, depth, number of subnetworks) to depend on smoothness α.
Proposition 4’s variational equivalence between weight‐decay objective and ℓₚ penalty is stated imprecisely.
Technical core is estimation‐error bound under fixed design (Theorem 5, Lemma 6); approximation error largely follows prior work.
technical_details: Covering‐number bound (Theorem 5) replaces dependence on M subnetworks with a bound on total Frobenius norm, but depends on unknown P′.
Proposition 4 claims equivalence between Eq. (2) and an ℓₚ‐penalized form, yet the mapping of regularization parameter λ to constraint P is not shown.
Theorem 1’s architecture requirements (width w=O(md), depth L, subnetworks M≈n^{(1−2/L)/(2α/d+1−2/(pL))}) grow exponentially with dimension d.
Estimation‐error analysis is carried out under a fixed‐design assumption, which the reviewer notes is significantly simpler than the random‐design case.
Approximation of Besov/BV classes uses B-spline based constructions following Yarotsky (2017) and Suzuki (2018).

title: Tensor-Based Sketching Method for the Low-Rank Approximation of Data Streams.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: In this work, the authors summarized previous algorithms to recover column/row spaces of a set of matrices fast and introduced Tucker decomposition of tensor into them, which can give a good choice of hyperparameter in those algorithms.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength: in those previous algorithms, they require the approximation for matrices in the training set to be as accurate as possible and this work provides the method based on Tucker decomposition with theoretical analysis. And the idea of viewing a stream of matrices as a tensor is novel and helpful in finding their common subspaces. 

Weakness: the analysis is not complete. It didn’t mention how close is S, the estimator of U_k, in general cases to U_k. Also the bound in theorem 2 is not clear with m and n.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: The quality is fine. Some definitions are not so clear, e.g. in (1.1) where the authors define B, is this B for all d, or it should be B_d for different d? It is pretty novel.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This work should qualify for a conference paper, although there are something which can be improved.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
In this work, the authors summarized previous algorithms to recover column/row spaces of a set of matrices fast and introduced Tucker decomposition of tensor into them, which can give a good choice of hyperparameter in those algorithms.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength: in those previous algorithms, they require the approximation for matrices in the training set to be as accurate as possible and this work provides the method based on Tucker decomposition with theoretical analysis. And the idea of viewing a stream of matrices as a tensor is novel and helpful in finding their common subspaces. 

Weakness: the analysis is not complete. It didn’t mention how close is S, the estimator of U_k, in general cases to U_k. Also the bound in theorem 2 is not clear with m and n.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The quality is fine. Some definitions are not so clear, e.g. in (1.1) where the authors define B, is this B for all d, or it should be B_d for different d? It is pretty novel.

Summary Of The Review:
This work should qualify for a conference paper, although there are something which can be improved.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper presents a novel sketching technique that treats a sequence of input matrices as a third–order tensor and applies a Tucker decomposition to extract a common subspace. The authors provide theoretical bounds (notably Theorem 2) to support the accuracy of their sketching matrix S, and they demonstrate empirical performance on streaming matrix data. Overall, the tensor‐based perspective and accompanying analysis represent a fresh contribution to the sketching literature, but certain theoretical and notational aspects require clarification before the work can be fully assessed.

Strengths  
1. Novel Tensor Viewpoint: Framing a stream of matrices as a third‐order tensor and using Tucker decomposition to find shared subspaces is an original and insightful idea.  
2. Theoretical Development: The paper does not stop at algorithmic proposals—it offers analysis (e.g., Theorem 2) to bound the sketching error, which is valuable for understanding when and why the method works.  
3. Practical Relevance: By sketching streaming data in tensor form, the method addresses realistic scenarios in large‐scale data processing and subspace tracking.

Weaknesses  
1. Incomplete Accuracy Analysis: While the paper introduces estimator S for the true subspace U_k, it stops short of a general discussion or bound on how closely S approximates U_k across different settings. It is unclear what guarantees hold beyond the specific cases considered.  
2. Unspecified Dimensional Dependencies: Theorem 2 provides an error bound but does not explicitly state how this bound scales with the number of rows m and columns n of the input matrices. Without this, it is difficult to judge how the method will perform as data dimensions grow.  
3. Notational Ambiguities: Equation (1.1) introduces a matrix B but does not clarify whether B_d varies with each slice d or remains constant across all slices. This ambiguity hinders reproducibility and clarity.

Detailed Suggestions  
• Quantify Subspace Approximation: Provide a general theorem or corollary that explicitly relates the distance ∥(I–SSᵀ)U_k∥ to problem parameters, thereby quantifying how closely S captures U_k in expectation or with high probability.  
• State Dimensional Dependence: In the statement of Theorem 2 (and any other error bounds), include explicit factors of m and n (and possibly rank k) to clarify how the bound scales with the size of the input matrices.  
• Refine Notation in Eq. (1.1): Introduce distinct symbols for B_d (the dth factor matrix) if it varies with d, or state clearly that B is fixed across all slices. A brief notation table early in the manuscript may help readers follow the tensor construction more easily.

Minor Comments  
– It would help to include a brief discussion comparing your bound in Theorem 2 to existing matrix‐sketching guarantees, to put your results in context.  
– Ensure consistent use of transpose notation (e.g., sometimes written as Sᵀ and other times as S^T).

Decision  
Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)  
I recommend acceptance, contingent on clarifying the theoretical completeness (subspace‐approximation bounds), making the dependence on m and n explicit in Theorem 2, and tightening the notation around B versus B_d. These revisions will substantially strengthen the paper’s clarity and impact.

And the idea of viewing a stream of matrices as a tensor is novel and helpful in finding their common subspaces.

Summary Of The Review:
This work should qualify for a conference paper, although there are something which can be improved.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper studies the data-driven low-rank approximation problem on data streams, which has gained attention recently. For a single matrix, some fast algorithms for low-rank approximation are based on sketching, and specifically they choose a sparse sketching matrix with random signs, which is multiplied by the original matrix to reduce its row and/or column dimensions while still allowing approximate recovery of the best low-rank approximation. In a stream of matrices assumed to be related to each other, it has been recently shown that the entries of the sketching matrix can be chosen in a data-driven way based on matrices previously seen on the stream instead of using random signs, in order to improve performance.

This paper suggests a new algorithm along these lines, that views the stream of matrices as a tensor of order 3, which leads to a formulation of a loss problem that can be solved directly and yields an appropriate sketching matrix, albeit non-sparse.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
The tensor-based approach seems interesting and may lead to useful insights on the problem. However, there is a major issue that makes the proposed algorithm incomparable to prior work, both theoretically and empirically - the lack of sparsity of the output sketching matrix. This unfortunately renders the paper methodologically unviable. 

In all prior work (both oblivious and data-driven), the sketching matrix has a certain structure that allows fast matrix multiplication by it (usually sparsity), while here no such structure is guaranteed. On the theoretical level, this makes the resulting low-rank approximation algorithm asymptotically slower than all of the prior work it is compared to.

The manuscript is aware of this issue, and finally addresses it on the empirical level, on page 8, stating that even though the asymptotic running time is slower, the proposed algorithm is in fact empirically faster than prior work based on experiments. It goes on to explain this is because the implementation of the proposed algorithm uses the built-in (highly optimized) PyTorch matrix multiplication functionality, while the baselines were implemented with the non-optimized (and inevitably much slower) sparse matrix multiplication code released by the authors of those works. This unfortunately renders the reported testing time results essentially meaningless: the algorithms should be compared in the same experimental setting (software and hardware). Using the same dense matrix multiplication functionality in PyTorch for the sparse baselines would have already improved their running time to the same as the proposed method, erasing any reported advantage in testing time.

To be clear, it is true that as of now, PyTorch and comparable optimized software packages for matrices do not exploit sparsity, and dense matrix multiplication is as fast as sparse on the datasets considered here. Sparsity is useful theoretically (for getting better asymptotic bounds) and also in practice if the matrices are too large to fit in working memory (which makes dense matrix multiplication slow or infeasible). I do not question the reported results themselves, and one could perhaps make the argument that the sparsity of the sketching matrix is not helpful at all and could be dispensed with, but the current framing and methodological design of the paper are unviable. If sparsity is indeed unhelpful, then the sparse baseline should be implemented as dense ones and not crippled by attempting to leverage sparsity, and dense methods for low-rank approximation could be considered and reported as well (even full SVD).

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper studies the data-driven low-rank approximation problem on data streams, which has gained attention recently. For a single matrix, some fast algorithms for low-rank approximation are based on sketching, and specifically they choose a sparse sketching matrix with random signs, which is multiplied by the original matrix to reduce its row and/or column dimensions while still allowing approximate recovery of the best low-rank approximation. In a stream of matrices assumed to be related to each other, it has been recently shown that the entries of the sketching matrix can be chosen in a data-driven way based on matrices previously seen on the stream instead of using random signs, in order to improve performance.

This paper suggests a new algorithm along these lines, that views the stream of matrices as a tensor of order 3, which leads to a formulation of a loss problem that can be solved directly and yields an appropriate sketching matrix, albeit non-sparse.

Strength And Weaknesses:
The tensor-based approach seems interesting and may lead to useful insights on the problem. However, there is a major issue that makes the proposed algorithm incomparable to prior work, both theoretically and empirically - the lack of sparsity of the output sketching matrix. This unfortunately renders the paper methodologically unviable. 

In all prior work (both oblivious and data-driven), the sketching matrix has a certain structure that allows fast matrix multiplication by it (usually sparsity), while here no such structure is guaranteed. On the theoretical level, this makes the resulting low-rank approximation algorithm asymptotically slower than all of the prior work it is compared to.

The manuscript is aware of this issue, and finally addresses it on the empirical level, on page 8, stating that even though the asymptotic running time is slower, the proposed algorithm is in fact empirically faster than prior work based on experiments. It goes on to explain this is because the implementation of the proposed algorithm uses the built-in (highly optimized) PyTorch matrix multiplication functionality, while the baselines were implemented with the non-optimized (and inevitably much slower) sparse matrix multiplication code released by the authors of those works. This unfortunately renders the reported testing time results essentially meaningless: the algorithms should be compared in the same experimental setting (software and hardware). Using the same dense matrix multiplication functionality in PyTorch for the sparse baselines would have already improved their running time to the same as the proposed method, erasing any reported advantage in testing time.

To be clear, it is true that as of now, PyTorch and comparable optimized software packages for matrices do not exploit sparsity, and dense matrix multiplication is as fast as sparse on the datasets considered here. Sparsity is useful theoretically (for getting better asymptotic bounds) and also in practice if the matrices are too large to fit in working memory (which makes dense matrix multiplication slow or infeasible). I do not question the reported results themselves, and one could perhaps make the argument that the sparsity of the sketching matrix is not helpful at all and could be dispensed with, but the current framing and methodological design of the paper are unviable. Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Clarity: I found the proofs abbreviated and difficult to follow, and it would help to expand them and add explanations and bridging steps.
Reproducibility: I appreciate the fully disclosed details of the implementation.

Summary Of The Review:
This is a potentially interesting paper but with flaws in its framing and methodological design, with which I cannot recommend acceptance. I could have recommended accepting a version of the paper with largely the same technical material, but that clarifies properly the departure from prior work, and with a viable experimental design that compares the baselines on a level ground and includes more relevant baselines. I also recommend rewriting the proofs in a clearer way that would assist in verifying the stated theorems.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents a novel tensor‐based, data‐driven approach to learning low‐rank sketching matrices by framing sketch design as a subspace identification problem. The authors provide full implementation details and demonstrate empirical speedups over classic sparse sketching methods.

Strengths  
• Novel formulation: Recasting sketching in a tensor framework is an interesting perspective that may yield new theoretical and practical insights.  
• Subspace viewpoint: The method connects sketching quality directly to the geometry of the data subspace, which is a fresh angle.  
• Reproducibility: All code and training routines are fully disclosed, making the work easy to reproduce and build upon.

Weaknesses and Major Concerns  
1. Density vs. Sparsity  
   The learned sketching matrix is fully dense, whereas prior sketching algorithms (e.g., Clarkson–Woodruff transforms) explicitly exploit sparsity to achieve fast matrix‐vector products. This fundamental shift means the proposed method cannot fairly be compared to sparse baselines on either theoretical or practical grounds.  
2. Asymptotic Complexity  
   Because of the dense multiplications, the asymptotic running time of the proposed algorithm is worse than that of classical sparse sketches. The paper does not address this theoretical shortcoming.  
3. Empirical Speedups Driven by Implementation Differences  
   The reported wall‐clock improvements arise from using heavily optimized dense PyTorch routines (torch.matmul) for the proposed method, while the sparse baselines rely on unoptimized sparse code paths. This discrepancy invalidates direct timing comparisons.  
4. Proof Exposition  
   The theoretical results are stated clearly, but their proofs are presented in overly abbreviated form. Critical intermediate steps are omitted, making it difficult to verify the claims.  
5. Experimental Fairness  
   Baselines are not evaluated under the same software and hardware conditions as the proposed method. Without re‐implementing other methods using the same optimized kernels or, conversely, testing the new method under sparse routines, the experimental conclusions remain uncertain.

Detailed Suggestions for Improvement  
• Fair Baseline Implementation: Re‐implement classical sparse sketching methods using the same dense matrix routines (e.g., torch.matmul) or accelerate baselines via torch.sparse.mm to ensure a level playing field in runtime comparisons.  
• Justify Sparsity Removal: Clarify why sparsity can be abandoned—if the dense approach truly outperforms sparse methods, provide theoretical or empirical evidence of this advantage.  
• Compare to Dense Low‐Rank Methods: If sparsity is not leveraged, include additional dense baselines such as full SVD, IVY, or FewShotSGD, which optimize all entries of the sketching matrix.  
• Expand Proofs: Rewrite and enlarge the proof sections, supplying missing lemmas and bridging arguments so that readers can follow each step without undue effort.  
• Standardize Experimental Protocol: Rerun all algorithms on identical hardware/software stacks, document versions, and ensure that matrix multiplications (sparse or dense) use equivalent levels of optimization.

Conclusion and Recommendation  
The paper offers an intriguing tensor‐based perspective and delivers valuable code for reproducibility. However, the central issue of unfair comparisons—stemming from the removal of sparsity and inconsistent implementations—must be addressed before the claimed speedups and asymptotic implications can be accepted. I believe that, with the suggested revisions, this work could make a solid contribution. At present, the paper sits marginally above the acceptance threshold. I therefore recommend a weak accept (Rating: 6), contingent on addressing the methodological and experimental fairness concerns.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper introduces a low-rank tensor approximation algorithm intended for data streams applications. 
According to the authors, the algorithm fully exploits the structure of data streams and obtains quasi-optimal sketching matrices by performing tensor decomposition on training data. 
The approach computes the sketching matrix by minimizing a new loss function which is a relaxation of that in the IVY tensor decomposition method. Specifically, the key is that the minimization of this loss function by tensor decomposition on the training set is non-iterative. The authors formulate a couple of theorems that bound the approximation performance and provide empirical analysis on several real data stream datasets.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
+ 

* The paper is well written and clear for the most part. It does need to be proof read for small typos and grammar.
*  The idea is well grounded in existing methods, e.g. IVY and SCW and the approximation quality is derived in two theorems.
*  The authors test their algorithm on real datasets and show some improvements. 

- 
* Overall I have some concerns regarding the novelty as the proposed algorithms are somewhat straightforward extensions of the existing algorithms, e.g. SCW.

reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper presents a non-iterative, tensor-decomposition-based sketching method for low-rank approximation in data streams. The authors derive two theoretical bounds on the approximation quality and demonstrate empirically that their approach outperforms several baseline sketching schemes on real datasets. The exposition is generally clear and well structured, and the theoretical and experimental contributions are solid. However, the novelty appears limited—largely a tensor-view extension of existing frameworks such as SCW—and the absence of released code raises reproducibility concerns. Overall, I lean marginally toward acceptance (rating 6) provided the authors address the issues below.

Strengths  
1. Clarity of Presentation: The manuscript is well written and organized. The description of the non-iterative Tucker decomposition and its application to sketching is accessible, aside from a handful of minor typos and grammatical slips.  
2. Theoretical Foundations: Building on IVY and SCW, the authors formulate a relaxed loss minimization whose solution via tensor decomposition yields a sketching matrix. Two new theorems rigorously bound the approximation error, reinforcing the soundness of the approach.  
3. Empirical Validation: Experiments on multiple real-world datasets show consistent accuracy improvements over standard baselines, validating the practical benefit of the proposed sketch.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited Novelty: Although the tensor perspective is interesting, the core algorithm is a relatively straightforward extension of the classical SCW randomized SVD framework, now recast via a Tucker decomposition. This raises questions about the paper’s originality.  
2. Reproducibility: The manuscript lacks any reference to released code or plans for open-sourcing the implementation, which hinders reproducibility and broader adoption.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Proofreading: Perform a careful pass to correct minor typographical and grammatical errors to further polish the exposition.  
2. Clarify Novel Contributions: More explicitly highlight aspects of the method that go beyond a direct tensor-based rephrasing of existing sketching algorithms. For example, emphasize any novel insights in the derivation of the relaxed loss or in the practical advantages of the non-iterative Tucker step.  
3. Code Release: Provide the implementation (or include a clear timeline for its release) to address reproducibility concerns. This could be as simple as a public repository with example scripts and datasets.

Conclusion  
The paper offers a clean, theoretically justified, non-iterative tensor approach to sketching that shows promising empirical performance. Despite reservations about its incremental novelty and the lack of shared code, the work makes a contribution worthy of publication after minor revisions. I therefore recommend acceptance with a marginal rating of 6.

The new optimization problem based on tensor decomposition can be seen as a relaxation of the original LRA problem. The experimental result suggests that the proposed algorithm has a lower error while having a faster training time.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength

1. The paper proposes an algorithm from a new insight, which has not been considered in the literature on the learning-based LRA. The paper also gives a theoretical guarantee.

2. The experimental results are strong and interesting. The proposed algorithm has a lower error while having a faster training time. 

Weakness

0. Note that the template of this paper is actually a little different than the ICLR template.
1. The analysis of the theorem is straightforward. Besides, the Theorem 2 seems only to give an additive error $\epsilon ||A||_F^2$,  I am wondering that would it be possible to get a multiplicative error $\epsilon ||A- A_k||_F^2$, which is the common setting in the literature of the sketching-based LRA.
2. As mentioned in the paper, the new learning-based sketch matrix is a dense matrix, which I think is the main drawback of this paper. The dense sketch matrix seems not to be useful when the size of the data is large. Note that if $S$ is a Count-Sketch matrix, we can compute $SA$ in $nnz(A)$ time. That is one of the reasons that most of the previous works only study the sparse sketching matrix.  As mentioned in the work of IVY19, One applications scenario of the learning-based sketch involves processing streams of data (video, data logs, customer activity etc) by executing the same algorithm on an hourly, daily or weekly basis. Hence in this sense the testing time is more important if the training time is only a little slower as the training process can be done before the new data comes. Also, notice that the sparse matrix has the pass-efficiency property when the data size exceeds memory available in RAM, which is what the author mentioned in the conclusion section. The update time for one update of the Count-Sketch matrix is $O(1)$.

3. The paper mentions the testing time in the experiment section but it seems not to be done in the correct way. It says in the paper that "have no idea how to accelerate the matrix multiplication SA while exploiting the sparse structure of S". However, suppose that the position and value vectors are $p$, $v$. Note that if the $t$-th non-zero entries of $A$ is $A_{ij} = c$, we just need to do the update 
$
SA[p_i, j] = SA[p_i, j]  + v_i \cdot c . 
$
which means the time to compute $SA$ is the same as to read the matrix $A$. 

4. The authors mention the memory usage in the experiment section. Can the author give a detailed result of the memory usage for all approaches?

I can raise the score if the author can address this adequately (note that the sparsity property is not the only way to compute $SA$ fast, like the Subsampled Randomized Hadamard Transform(SRHT) matrix is a dense sketching matrix while we can compute $SA$ in $O(nd \log n)$ time) .
