reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents a novel learning‐based low‐rank approximation framework that leverages a Tucker tensor relaxation combined with a random sketching operator. The authors derive theoretical guarantees relating their tensor‐based formulation to the original matrix approximation objective and report strong empirical performance—achieving lower approximation error and faster training than competing baselines.  

Strengths  
1. The proposed approach introduces a fresh perspective by applying Tucker tensor decomposition within a learning‐based sketching framework, an angle not previously explored in the literature on low‐rank approximation.  
2. The authors provide clear theoretical analysis, including a proof that their tensor‐based relaxation yields an additive error bound relative to the Frobenius norm of the input matrix.  
3. Experimental results demonstrate that the method consistently outperforms standard sketching and deterministic baselines, achieving both lower reconstruction error and faster training times in the tested regimes.  

Weaknesses  
1. Formatting and Presentation: The manuscript deviates from the standard ICLR template and lacks several presentation conventions expected at this venue, which hinders readability and consistency.  
2. Nature of the Error Bound: Theorem 2 offers only an additive error guarantee of the form O(ε)· ||A||_F^2, whereas much of the sketching literature focuses on stronger multiplicative bounds such as ε·||A − A_k||_F^2. This limits the theoretical impact of the result.  
3. Dense Sketching Matrix: The sketch matrix S used throughout is dense, making the approach impractical for streaming or large‐scale datasets. Dense sketches prevent the exploitation of one‐pass algorithms and impede efficient updates when A cannot fit entirely in memory.  
4. Flawed Testing Time Evaluation: In the experimental section, testing times are measured via a dense matrix multiplication implementation. This setup fails to leverage any potential sparse structure in S, exaggerating the claimed efficiency.  
5. Missing Memory‐Usage Comparison: The paper does not report detailed memory‐footprint measurements for the proposed method versus baselines, leaving open questions about its scalability in memory‐constrained environments.  

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Strengthen Theoretical Guarantees: Develop a multiplicative error bound of the form ε·||A − A_k||_F^2 to bring the theoretical contribution in line with standard sketching results.  
2. Investigate Sparse or Structured Sketches: Replace or augment the dense sketch matrix with a sparse alternative such as Count‐Sketch or a structured transform like the SRHT. These sketches admit O(nnz(A)) time updates and constant‐time per entry adjustments, making the approach viable for streaming data.  
3. Correct Testing‐Time Measurements: Re‐implement the evaluation of SA in a sparse fashion—e.g., using position/value vectors for Count‐Sketch or torch.sparse.mm for structured transforms—to obtain realistic timing results.  
4. Include Memory Profiling: Provide a comprehensive comparison of peak memory usage across all methods, demonstrating how the proposed approach scales relative to dense and sparse baselines.  
5. Adhere to Conference Formatting: Conform to the ICLR style guidelines to improve clarity and facilitate reader comprehension.  

Minor Comments  
- In Section 3.2, it would help to explicitly contrast the additive bound in Theorem 2 with the more common multiplicative guarantees.  
- A brief discussion of the implications for out‐of‐core or pass‐efficient algorithms (when A exceeds main memory) would underscore the practical limitations of a dense sketch matrix.  

Decision  
Rating: 5 – marginally below the acceptance threshold.  
While the paper offers a novel tensor‐based perspective and shows promising empirical results, the limitations in theoretical strength, practical sketching implementation, and experimental evaluation must be addressed before it can be considered for acceptance.

title: EAGLE: Large-scale Learning of Turbulent Fluid Dynamics with Mesh Transformers

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper introduces a dataset for predicting fluid turbulences and a method using a Transformer to learn the dynamics. It adds clustering and graph pooling before the attention block so that the receptive field is not constrained in a local neighborhood. Results show that the new method outperforms the previous works.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:
1. Although different shapes of the boundary affect how the mesh is constructed and how they are clustered, it seems that the model still generalizes well.
2. The results are promising and supportive of the technical contribution.

Weakness:
1. Missing reference: Takahashi, Tetsuya, et al. "Differentiable fluids with solid coupling for learning and control." Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence. Vol. 35. No. 7. 2021.
2. I wonder how this can be applied to 3D cases. Since the 2D task only there is already 270Gb data, it is probably not practical to prepare a good amount of data for the model to converge and generalize. Also, dealing with tetrahedra instead of triangles may introduce unknown challenges. Please add more insights regarding the extension to 3D in the conclusion section.
3. How does the mesh resolution affect the training/test accuracy? If the training and test images are of different resolutions, will the model perform better or worse? It would be great to see such comparisons in the experiments.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: See above.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: I would recommend weak reject because of the weaknesses described above. I am happy to change my score if the issues are addressed in the rebuttal.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper introduces a dataset for predicting fluid turbulences and a method using a Transformer to learn the dynamics. It adds clustering and graph pooling before the attention block so that the receptive field is not constrained in a local neighborhood. Results show that the new method outperforms the previous works.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength:
1. Although different shapes of the boundary affect how the mesh is constructed and how they are clustered, it seems that the model still generalizes well.
2. The results are promising and supportive of the technical contribution.

Weakness:
1. Missing reference: Takahashi, Tetsuya, et al. "Differentiable fluids with solid coupling for learning and control." Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence. Vol. 35. No. 7. 2021.
2. I wonder how this can be applied to 3D cases. Since the 2D task only there is already 270Gb data, it is probably not practical to prepare a good amount of data for the model to converge and generalize. Also, dealing with tetrahedra instead of triangles may introduce unknown challenges. Please add more insights regarding the extension to 3D in the conclusion section.
3. How does the mesh resolution affect the training/test accuracy? If the training and test images are of different resolutions, will the model perform better or worse? It would be great to see such comparisons in the experiments.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
See above.

Summary Of The Review:
I would recommend weak reject because of the weaknesses described above. I am happy to change my score if the issues are addressed in the rebuttal.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper presents a large-scale 2D fluid turbulence dataset (≈270 GB) alongside a novel mesh‐based transformer model that leverages clustering and graph pooling to predict fluid dynamics. The authors demonstrate state‐of‐the‐art performance and strong generalization across a variety of boundary shapes, suggesting their approach is both technically sound and practically useful for 2D simulations.

Strengths  
1. Generalization Across Boundaries: The transformer model maintains high accuracy even when the boundary geometry changes, indicating robustness to different domain shapes and clustering configurations.  
2. Promising Technical Contributions: The combination of spectral clustering, graph pooling, and self‐attention on meshes yields compelling results that outperform baselines and validate the paper’s core ideas.

Weaknesses  
1. Missing Related Work: An important recent contribution—Takahashi et al. “Differentiable fluids with solid coupling for learning and control” (AAAI 2021)—is not cited or discussed, despite its relevance to differentiable fluid‐solid interactions.  
2. 2D‐to‐3D Extension Unaddressed: While the paper focuses on 2D triangular meshes, it lacks any discussion on how the method and dataset might scale to realistic 3D cases, which would require handling tetrahedral meshes and substantially more data.  
3. Mesh Resolution Sensitivity: There is no analysis on how differences in mesh resolution between training and testing affect model performance, leaving open questions about convergence and generalizability under varying discretizations.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Include Missing Citation: Please add and discuss Takahashi et al. (AAAI 2021) on differentiable fluids with solid coupling. This will situate your method more clearly within the existing literature.  
2. Discuss 3D Scalability: Offer insights or preliminary thoughts on moving from 2D triangles to 3D tetrahedra—such as data storage requirements, computational costs, and potential architectural adaptations.  
3. Experiment on Resolution Variance: Provide either empirical results or at least a theoretical discussion on how training with one mesh resolution and testing on another influences accuracy and stability. Even a small ablation would strengthen your claims about generalization.

Conclusion and Recommendation  
Overall, this work provides a valuable dataset and a well‐designed transformer architecture, achieving strong results in 2D fluid turbulence prediction. However, before publication, the authors should address the missing related work, clarify the path to 3D applications, and analyze mesh resolution effects. Given the paper’s technical merit but identified gaps, I assign a rating of 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold. With revisions tackling the points above, this paper will make a solid contribution to the field.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposed a novel GNN architecture and benchmark dataset to analyze the 2D turbulent fluid dynamics caused by a single moving flow source. By utilizing the autoregressive model and self-attention modules, the proposed method achieved outperforming performance on the testing dataset.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength:
+ The paper is well written. Algorithms are well explained and easy to understand. 
+ The results are good, from both qualitative and quantitative perspective.
+ The experiments are comprehensive and well demonstrate the robustness of the method

Weakness:
- The dataset is simulated by some existing dynamic fluid simulator, which means that the predicted results are highly impact the the quality of the simulator. 
- Since there's no real experiments has been carried out and the analysis are based on 2D, I am not sure how and how well this model can work in the 3D real scene scenarios. It will be great if the authors can briefly discuss its applications or include some real results.
- It will be great if there's a failure case analysis or discussion.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
I believe that the overall quality of this paper is good.

Summary Of The Review:
In summary, I think this paper is well written and provide some solid results with respect to the 2D dynamic fluid analysis. I think it will be better if the authors can include a short paragraph discussing the real scene application and failure case discussion.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces a novel graph neural network (GNN) architecture for forecasting 2D turbulent fluid flows. The model combines an autoregressive framework with self-attention modules to capture spatiotemporal dependencies, and it is evaluated on a benchmark dataset generated by a dynamic fluid simulator. The authors report both qualitative visualizations and quantitative error metrics, demonstrating that their approach outperforms several baselines.

Strengths  
1. Clarity and Presentation: The manuscript is very well written. The problem formulation, model architecture, and training procedure are explained in a clear, step-by-step manner that is easy to follow.  
2. Strong Empirical Results: The method shows competitive performance on the simulated benchmark, with both visual examples and numerical metrics supporting its efficacy.  
3. Comprehensive Experiments: The authors conduct a range of ablations and sensitivity studies, illustrating the impact of key design choices (e.g., attention layers, autoregressive steps) and underscoring the model’s robustness.

Weaknesses  
1. Simulation Dependency: All experiments rely on data produced by an existing 2D fluid simulator. Consequently, the results may reflect simulator biases, and it is unclear how well the model would generalize if the simulator’s fidelity is imperfect.  
2. Limited to 2D and Synthetic Data: The paper does not include any real-world experiments or even simple 3D tests. This leaves the applicability of the proposed approach to practical fluid‐dynamics problems an open question.  
3. Lack of Failure Analysis: There is no dedicated discussion of cases where the model fails or produces degraded forecasts, which would help users understand its limitations.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Real-World or 3D Discussion: Even if new experiments are not feasible before publication, a brief discussion of how the architecture might extend to 3D flows or to data collected from physical experiments would strengthen the paper.  
2. Failure Case Analysis: Including a section (or appendix) that showcases typical failure modes—e.g., under extreme Reynolds numbers or in regions with sparse connectivity—would provide valuable insight into the method’s practical boundaries.

Decision  
I recommend a marginal acceptance (Rating: 6). The paper makes a meaningful methodological contribution with clear presentation and solid simulated results. However, addressing real-world validity and failure cases will be important in follow-up work.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This manuscript proposed a method to train neural networks to predict turbulent fluid dynamics. For the neural architecture, this manuscript introduced the concept of a transformer into the fluid dynamics field, which outperforms previous methods due to long-range receptive fields. To support the training of the mesh transformer, this manuscript generates a large-scale 2D aerodynamics dataset using a controlled drone. The experiments showed the superior performance of their method over several different baselines.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:
- The dataset is large-scale. It covers a big fraction of the interest of the physical datasets. Previous datasets are mostly steady-state simulations.
- The manuscript compared their method with several well-known baselines.

Weaknesses:
- In my opinion, the weaknesses are obvious. First, the dataset only contains 2D data, whose usefulness is suspicious due to the diffusion of fluid. I wonder if the authors can provide more insights on how they are useful in real engineering.
- Several previous datasets are working in CFD, e.g., http://turbulence.pha.jhu.edu/. How is the proposed dataset compared to theirs?
- The only variation in the dataset is the geometry. However, in reality, there are more descriptions of fluid (it is true even for air itself), e.g., the Reynolds number, the temperature, the boundary conditions, and the wind speed. I wonder how generalizable a network trained on this dataset can be.

Strength And Weaknesses:

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces a mesh-based transformer architecture designed to capture long-range receptive fields for unsteady fluid dynamics prediction, and pairs it with a newly released large-scale 2D aerodynamics dataset generated from controlled drone simulations. The authors demonstrate that their transformer outperforms several strong graph- and convolution-based baselines on this dataset, which covers a broader variety of geometries and unsteady conditions than prior steady-state collections. The manuscript is well written, clearly motivating the problem and detailing both the model design and dataset generation procedures.

Strengths  
1. Novel Architecture: The proposed transformer leverages global attention over mesh nodes, allowing the model to more effectively learn long-range interactions in turbulent flow compared to conventional GNNs or CNNs.  
2. Large-Scale 2D Dataset: The authors contribute a substantial 2D aerodynamics dataset simulating airflow around a moving drone over diverse ground geometries, filling a gap in unsteady flow benchmarks.  
3. Empirical Performance: Through comprehensive experiments against established baselines, the paper shows consistent improvements in predictive accuracy on unsteady turbulence tasks.  
4. Clarity and Presentation: The writing is crisp and well organized, with clear exposition of motivation, related work, methodology, and quantitative results.

Weaknesses  
1. 2D Scope: Restricting the dataset to two dimensions raises questions about the dataset’s direct relevance for real-world, inherently 3D fluid phenomena where diffusion and out-of-plane effects play a major role.  
2. Comparison to Established CFD Resources: The paper does not benchmark or contrast its dataset and tasks against widely used CFD repositories, such as the Johns Hopkins turbulence database, limiting the context for its contributions.  
3. Limited Fluid Parameter Variation: Apart from geometric diversity, the dataset omits variations in key parameters (Reynolds number, temperature, boundary conditions, wind speed), which are critical for general physical modeling.  
4. Generalization Unclear: It remains uncertain how a model trained exclusively on the proposed 2D dataset would transfer to more complex engineering scenarios involving full 3D turbulence and varying flow conditions.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Practical Relevance: Provide stronger justification or case studies that illustrate how insights from the 2D dataset can inform or accelerate design tasks in engineering practice.  
2. Dataset Comparison: Include a detailed comparison (quantitative or qualitative) between the new dataset and existing benchmarks (e.g., the Johns Hopkins turbulence database), highlighting differences in scale, parameter coverage, and intended use cases.  
3. Fluid Parameter Discussion: Discuss strategies for incorporating or simulating variations in Reynolds number, temperature, boundary conditions, and wind speed—either within the current dataset or in planned extensions.  
4. Generalization Study: Conduct preliminary experiments to assess how models trained on this 2D dataset perform on more complex or higher-fidelity simulations, or at least outline a roadmap for such evaluations in future work.

Overall Assessment  
This work makes a meaningful contribution by combining a novel mesh transformer for unsteady flow prediction with a sizable new dataset that extends beyond traditional steady-state benchmarks. While questions remain regarding 3D applicability and parameter coverage, the paper nonetheless pushes the frontier on data-driven simulation of turbulent flows. Given its strengths in methodology, dataset scale, and empirical validation—and noting that the weaknesses can be addressed with additional analysis or discussion—I recommend a marginal acceptance (rating: 6) pending minor revisions.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper makes two contributions: First, it presents a large-scale 2D fluid simulation dataset called EAGLE. The dataset consists of 2D scenes with (dynamically changing) rigid boundaries and Reynold-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) fluids. Second, it proposes a neural network model (mesh transformer) for solving fluid problems and compares its performance with popular neural network simulators (MeshGraphNet, etc).
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
The proposal of a large-scale fluid simulation dataset is very timely. I believe having a high-quality dataset will foster relevant research in the learning community.

On the other hand, part of me feels this dataset is still premature. It is probably better than many existing fluid simulation datasets in previous papers, but I think it could have been more polished.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
There are quite a few things unclear to me in the simulation dataset:
- Are there any reasons behind the choice of simulating RANS instead of the original Navier-Stokes equations in the dataset?
- It looks like the drone and the fluid are one-way coupled instead of two-way coupled: the drone provides boundary conditions to the fluid’s motion, but the fluid does not affect the drone’s motion (there are no velocity or pressure terms in Eqn. 8). If the fluid has no influence on the drone, I feel the MPC control is a bit redundant and a kinematic drone should suffice.
- I am not sure I get how the drone’s mesh serves as the boundary condition of the fluid area. It looks like its bounding box is used. Could you elaborate on the velocity/pressure boundary conditions around the bounding boxes? I was expecting some inlet/outlet flow velocity to be set at where the propellers are, but I didn’t find details about it.
- I don’t quite understand the task definition at the end of Sec. 3. Do the node positions x_i change over time? It looks like the fluid area is remeshed at every frame if I am not mistaken.

I initially thought the proposed dataset simulated two-way coupled Navier-Stokes fluids and a dynamic, controllable UAV when reading the main text and was quite excited. It would be good if the paper could clarify the setting upfront in Sec. 3.

Regarding the network model, it looks like the velocity and pressure fields are predicted purely based on the original mesh at the current time followed by resampled to the new mesh if I am not mistaken. This seems questionable to me because I think the correct velocity and pressure field at the next time step will be determined by both the current and new mesh.

Reproducibility: I guess this largely depends on what will be included in the released dataset. Replicating the full simulation results using Ansys is probably intractable for people with limited access to computational resources, but I still suggest the dataset include configurations of the scenes in Ansys if possible.

Other comments:
- Is EAGLE an acronym?
- The baselines are confusing: the beginning of Sec. 5 refers MeshGraphNet and Cylinder-Flow to Li et al. 2019, but I don’t think that paper means either of them. By MeshGraphNet the paper probably means Pfaff et al. [2021].
- I appreciate that the paper frankly talked about failure cases and poor results (Scalar flow) in the experiments.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Currently, I am leaning toward rejection, although my score is not final yet. It is definitely a respectable submission, and I welcome different opinions from the authors and other reviewers.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper makes two contributions: First, it presents a large-scale 2D fluid simulation dataset called EAGLE. The dataset consists of 2D scenes with (dynamically changing) rigid boundaries and Reynold-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) fluids. Second, it proposes a neural network model (mesh transformer) for solving fluid problems and compares its performance with popular neural network simulators (MeshGraphNet, etc).

Strength And Weaknesses:
The proposal of a large-scale fluid simulation dataset is very timely. I believe having a high-quality dataset will foster relevant research in the learning community.

On the other hand, part of me feels this dataset is still premature. It is probably better than many existing fluid simulation datasets in previous papers, but I think it could have been more polished.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
There are quite a few things unclear to me in the simulation dataset:
- Are there any reasons behind the choice of simulating RANS instead of the original Navier-Stokes equations in the dataset?
- It looks like the drone and the fluid are one-way coupled instead of two-way coupled: the drone provides boundary conditions to the fluid’s motion, but the fluid does not affect the drone’s motion (there are no velocity or pressure terms in Eqn. 8). If the fluid has no influence on the drone, I feel the MPC control is a bit redundant and a kinematic drone should suffice.
- I am not sure I get how the drone’s mesh serves as the boundary condition of the fluid area. It looks like its bounding box is used. Could you elaborate on the velocity/pressure boundary conditions around the bounding boxes? I was expecting some inlet/outlet flow velocity to be set at where the propellers are, but I didn’t find details about it.
- I don’t quite understand the task definition at the end of Sec. 3. Do the node positions x_i change over time? It looks like the fluid area is remeshed at every frame if I am not mistaken.

I initially thought the proposed dataset simulated two-way coupled Navier-Stokes fluids and a dynamic, controllable UAV when reading the main text and was quite excited. It would be good if the paper could clarify the setting upfront in Sec. 3.

Regarding the network model, it looks like the velocity and pressure fields are predicted purely based on the original mesh at the current time followed by resampled to the new mesh if I am not mistaken. This seems questionable to me because I think the correct velocity and pressure field at the next time step will be determined by both the current and new mesh.

Reproducibility: I guess this largely depends on what will be included in the released dataset. Replicating the full simulation results using Ansys is probably intractable for people with limited access to computational resources, but I still suggest the dataset include configurations of the scenes in Ansys if possible.

Other comments:
- Is EAGLE an acronym?
- The baselines are confusing: the beginning of Sec. 5 refers MeshGraphNet and Cylinder-Flow to Li et al. 2019, but I don’t think that paper means either of them. By MeshGraphNet the paper probably means Pfaff et al. [2021].
- I appreciate that the paper frankly talked about failure cases and poor results (Scalar flow) in the experiments.

Summary Of The Review:
Currently, I am leaning toward rejection, although my score is not final yet. It is definitely a respectable submission, and I welcome different opinions from the authors and other reviewers.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This work proposes a consolidator that modifies the pretrained model with the addition of a small set of tunable parameters to store the task-specific knowledge while freezing the backbone model during adaptation. A two-stage consolidation process is designed by merging corresponding parameters in the training-storage phase and loading-inference phase. Extensive experiments are conducted on various downstream tasks and the results outperform state-of-the-art methods with fewer stored parameters but superior performance.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:

(1) The authors propose a basic module, a consolidator for efficient transfer learning.

(2) A two-stage consolidation process is designed. 

(3) Extensive experiments on various downstream tasks show state-of-the-art performance.

Weaknesses:
This work aims to design a mergeable adapter for efficient transfer learning and proposes a two-stage consolidation process by merging corresponding parameters. I have some questions as follows. 

(1)  In Figure 1, the authors split channels and use zero padding, which might cause a heavy computational cost. Thus, the training time/speed should be shown for comparison with other tuning methods. Besides, I notice that the authors insert a consolidator in the FC layer in Figure 1. Will performance be further enhanced if the consolidator is inserted in other locations? Some recent tuning methods [1, 2] should also be discussed and compared in Table 1. 

(2)  Some other tuning baselines are missing in Table 4. The authors should conduct more experiments with other tuning methods for fair comparisons. 

(3)  The consolidator is designed for a linear layer. Can it be applied to other operational structures, such as self attention. Further, the current consolidator module is designed mainly in the vision transformer. In Table 1, Table 4 and Table 5, the proposed method obtains promising performance compared to the Full tuning. Push it forward, can it be extended to other model families such as CNNs or MLP architectures [3,4,5]? These will be interesting experiments. Due to the rebuttal time constraints, the authors do not need to add these extension experiments, but feel free to discuss them. 

(4)  There exists a large number of typos in the current version, the authors are able to polish it further. 

[1] ‘AdaptFormer: Adapting Vision Transformers for Scalable Visual Recognition’, in NeurIPS2022. 

[2] ‘Scaling & Shifting Your Features: A New Baseline for Efficient Model Tuning’, in NeurIPS2022. 

[3] ‘A ConvNet for the 2020s’, in CVPR2022.

[4] ‘AS-MLP: An Axial Shifted MLP Architecture for Vision’, in ICLR2022.

[5] ‘RepMLPNet: Hierarchical Vision MLP with Re-parameterized Locality’, in CVPR2022.

It outperforms state-of-the-art methods with fewer stored parameters but superior performance. I tend to vote marginally above the acceptance threshold. Some additional experiments should be conducted to further improve the quality of this manuscript.

This work proposes a consolidator that modifies the pretrained model with the addition of a small set of tunable parameters to store the task-specific knowledge while freezing the backbone model during adaptation. Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
It should not be hard to reproduce this work because the authors describe the specific details for training and hyper parameters. The authors also propose consolidation process, which is also original in efficient transfer learning.

Summary Of The Review:
This work proposes a two-stage consolidation process for efficient transfer learning. It outperforms state-of-the-art methods with fewer stored parameters but superior performance. I tend to vote marginally above the acceptance threshold. Some additional experiments should be conducted to further improve the quality of this manuscript.

8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces a novel “consolidator” module—essentially a mergeable adapter—for efficient transfer learning. The key idea is a two‐stage consolidation process: during fine‐tuning, the module’s channel‐split adapters are trained and stored separately, and at inference time they are merged via zero padding into the backbone weights. Experiments on a suite of vision transfer tasks demonstrate that this approach matches or exceeds state‐of‐the‐art performance while only storing a small fraction of additional parameters per task.

Strengths  
1. Novel Mergeable Adapter. The paper proposes a clean, intuitive “consolidator” design that can be merged back into pre-trained weights, removing the need to carry separate adapter parameters at inference.  
2. Two‐Stage Consolidation Process. The separation of training/storage and loading/inference stages is well motivated and technically sound, enabling a lightweight deployment.  
3. Strong Empirical Results. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets show that the consolidator achieves superior transfer accuracy compared to existing parameter-efficient tuning methods, despite storing far fewer extra parameters.

Weaknesses  
1. Computational Overhead. The channel‐splitting and zero‐padding operations illustrated in Figure 1 may introduce nontrivial runtime cost, but no analysis of training or inference speed is provided.  
2. Limited Insertion Points. All evaluations insert the consolidator only into fully‐connected layers. It remains unclear whether earlier layers, convolutional blocks, or self‐attention modules would yield comparable gains.  
3. Missing Baselines. Key recent tuning methods such as AdaptFormer and SSF (Scaling & Shifting) are neither discussed in the text nor included in Tables 1 and 4, hindering a fair apples‐to‐apples comparison.  
4. Table 4 Omissions. Several established parameter-efficient baselines are absent, which undercuts the claim of state‐of-the-art performance.  
5. Scope of Applicability. The current focus is on linear layers within vision transformers; extension to CNNs, MLP stacks, or self‐attention remains unexplored.  
6. Writing Quality. The manuscript contains numerous typos and phrasing issues that occasionally obscure technical points.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Runtime Analysis. Report wall‐clock training times and inference latencies alongside accuracy results to quantify any computational overhead introduced by channel splitting and zero padding.  
2. Expanded Evaluations. Experiment with inserting the consolidator at other network locations—e.g., convolutional layers in CNNs or self‐attention heads in transformers—to test the generality of the approach.  
3. Include Recent Baselines. Add AdaptFormer, SSF (Scaling & Shifting), and other recent tuning methods into Tables 1 and 4 to ensure fair benchmarking against the latest techniques.  
4. Broader Architecture Tests. Either discuss or empirically validate how the consolidator could be adapted to MLP blocks, convolutional kernels, and self‐attention modules.  
5. Manuscript Polishing. Thoroughly proofread the paper to eliminate typographical and grammatical errors, and improve overall clarity.

Conclusion  
Overall, this work presents a compelling and practically useful method for parameter-efficient transfer learning. While there are open questions regarding computational cost, broader applicability, and comparison with the very latest baselines, these do not detract from the paper’s core contributions. I recommend acceptance. Rating: 8 (accept, good paper).

It should not be hard to reproduce this work because the authors describe the specific details for training and hyper parameters. The authors also propose consolidation process, which is also original in efficient transfer learning.

Summary Of The Review:
This work proposes a two-stage consolidation process for efficient transfer learning. It outperforms state-of-the-art methods with fewer stored parameters but superior performance. I tend to vote marginally above the acceptance threshold. Some additional experiments should be conducted to further improve the quality of this manuscript.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents “Consolidator,” a mergeable, multi-branch adapter for parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large vision models. The method organizes adapter weights into two stages—training/storage and loading/inference—enabling high adaptation capacity with no extra inference cost. The implementation is clearly described, and the write-up is generally well structured.

Strengths  
1. The proposed multi-branch consolidator is highly parameter-efficient and can be merged back into the base model for inference, providing flexibility without runtime overhead.  
2. The two-stage consolidation process (training→storage vs. loading→inference) is a neat design that maximizes adaptation capacity while avoiding additional inference cost.  
3. The presentation is clear and well written, with helpful diagrams and an accessible description of the training and merge procedures.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited novelty: The mergeable adapter concept closely resembles prior work such as LoRA. It is not sufficiently clarified what the fundamental innovation is beyond re-organizing adapters into branches.  
2. Channel Reorder ambiguity: The paper introduces a “Channel Reorder” operation but does not clearly distinguish it from the Shuffle operator used in ShuffleNet. It is unclear whether this is a known technique under a new name.  
3. Incomplete related work discussion: Several relevant recent methods—e.g., visual prompt tuning (Bahng et al. 2022), AdaptFormer (Chen et al. 2022), and convolutional bypass adapters (Jie & Deng 2022)—are omitted.  
4. Marginal empirical gains: On benchmarks, the improvement over the state-of-the-art NOAH is small, calling into question the practical significance of the added complexity.  
5. Reproducibility concerns: The authors have not released source code, hindering reproducibility of results.  
6. Minor typo: In Section 3.2, “aim ti” should be corrected to “aim to.”

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Clearly articulate the novel contributions beyond those already introduced by LoRA. Emphasize what in the branch-merging mechanism confers a distinct advantage.  
• Explicitly compare Channel Reorder to the ShuffleNet shuffle operator: detail any algorithmic or functional differences.  
• Expand the related work section to discuss and compare with recent parameter-efficient tuning methods such as Bahng et al. (2022), Chen et al. (2022), and Jie & Deng (2022).  
• Provide source code and instructions to facilitate reproducibility.  
• If possible, include an ablation or study demonstrating that the mergeable structure itself (rather than hyperparameter tuning) drives the performance gains over NOAH.

Decision  
Rating: 5 – marginally below the acceptance threshold  
While the paper is well written and offers a practical, parameter-efficient mechanism for fine-tuning, the novelty appears limited given close similarities to existing mergeable adapter approaches, the empirical gains are small, and key comparisons and clarifications are missing. Consequently, I cannot recommend acceptance in its current form.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper presents a new fine-tuning method for vision transformers that only trains and stores very few parameters. The fine-tuning method achieves both efficiency and effectiveness through its design of mergeable multiple branches and parameter shifting. Extensive experiments show the method surpasses other adapter methods and full fine-tuning on several transfer learning tasks.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:
1. Consolidator is a very effective method. Compared with other adapter methods and full parameter fine-tuning methods, Consolidator can achieve an almost optimal transfer learning effect.
2. Compared to the adapter methods, the design in this paper can be merged with the original model so that the inference phase does not increase the cost of memory and computation time.

Weakness:
1. The prior of ChannelReorder operation is strong but the explanation of its effectiveness is weak. 
2. The parameters in all tables are stored parameters for Consolidator but not trainable parameters.
3. It should be possible to save time compared to the full parameter finetune during training, I wonder why this is not reported, nor is it compared to other baseline training time.
4. The value of saving storage space is not so great, especially for the base-level model, and there are few experiments on the Large model.
5. There are slightly more spelling errors and grammatical errors. P3 workss; P4 ti, thw; P5 the all the; P7 accomodate.

reviews.text:
Review for “Consolidator: Mergeable Multi-Branch Adapters for Vision Transformers”

Summary  
This paper introduces Consolidator, a novel adapter-based fine-tuning framework for vision transformers. By employing a mergeable multi-branch architecture, Consolidator claims to achieve transfer learning performance on par with full fine-tuning and existing adapter methods, while incurring no additional inference-time memory or computational overhead. The core innovation is the ChannelReorder operation, which is asserted to inject a favorable inductive bias. Empirically, the method delivers near-optimal accuracy across a suite of downstream vision tasks using only a small fraction of the parameters. 

Strengths  
1. Transfer Learning Performance: Consolidator consistently matches or slightly exceeds the accuracy of full fine-tuning and other adapter approaches, demonstrating its effectiveness in low-parameter regimes.  
2. Inference Efficiency: Thanks to its mergeable multi-branch design, the approach adds zero extra memory or FLOPs at inference time, making it appealing for deployment.

Weaknesses  
1. Unclear Rationale for ChannelReorder: While the authors argue that ChannelReorder provides a strong inductive prior, the paper lacks a detailed theoretical or empirical analysis explaining why this operation boosts performance.  
2. Ambiguous Parameter Reporting: All of the tables report the total stored parameters for Consolidator rather than the actual number of parameters that are being updated during fine-tuning. This distinction between stored vs. trainable parameters must be clarified.  
3. Missing Training Time Comparisons: The manuscript does not provide any training time measurements for Consolidator relative to full fine-tuning or other adapters. Without these numbers, an important aspect of efficiency remains unquantified.  
4. Limited Large-Scale Evaluation: Storage savings on base-scale models are reported to be under 1%, which may be too small to be practically significant. Moreover, experiments on larger ViT variants are sparse, leaving open questions about the method’s scalability.  
5. Presentation Quality: Multiple spelling and grammatical errors (e.g., “workss” on p.3; “ti”, “thw” on p.4; “the all the” on p.5; “accomodate” on p.7) detract from the manuscript’s readability.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Strengthen the justification for ChannelReorder by:  
   a. Providing a formal analysis or intuitive explanation of how it influences feature representations.  
   b. Conducting ablation studies that isolate its effect under different settings.  
2. In tables and the surrounding text, explicitly distinguish between:  
   – Stored parameters (total number of adapter weights saved)  
   – Trainable parameters (weights updated during fine-tuning)  
3. Measure and report training speed (e.g., epochs or wall-clock time to convergence) for Consolidator, full fine-tuning, and other adapter baselines.  
4. Expand experiments to larger ViT architectures (e.g., ViT-Large or ViT-Huge) to demonstrate whether storage and performance gains hold at scale.  
5. Thoroughly proofread the manuscript to correct all spelling and grammar issues, improving overall clarity.

Recommendation  
The proposed Consolidator framework shows strong empirical promise by matching full fine-tuning performance with minimal inference overhead. However, the current manuscript falls marginally short of the bar due to insufficient explanation of key design choices, ambiguous metrics reporting, lack of training-time analysis, limited large-model experiments, and avoidable writing errors. I therefore recommend a rating of 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold.

reviews.text:
This paper tackles the important problem of adapting pre‐trained vision transformers for deployment on resource‐constrained edge devices. The authors propose a scheme of grouped connections combined with a re-parameterization strategy during training, and they demonstrate both improved accuracy and reduced compute cost over several baseline methods across multiple downstream vision tasks. Ablation studies are provided to isolate the contributions of each component.

Strengths  
1. Empirical Gains: The method yields consistent accuracy improvements while lowering inference FLOPs compared to baselines, indicating practical benefits for lightweight vision transformer adaptation.  
2. Ablation Analysis: The paper includes thorough ablation experiments that clarify how grouped connections and re-parameterization each contribute to overall performance.

Weaknesses  
1. Questionable Application Scenario: The paper motivates its approach by targeting edge devices that can perform training but allegedly suffer from storage constraints. However, it is unclear why such devices would have sufficient compute resources to fine-tune a large transformer yet lack adequate storage for a few extra parameters. This practical scenario requires stronger justification or real‐world evidence.  
2. Transformer-Specific Novelty: Although the authors frame their work as an adaptation strategy for transformers, the core idea—grouped connections plus re-parameterization—appears very similar to generic model re-parameterization techniques. The manuscript does not clearly explain what aspects of transformers (e.g., self-attention structure) are being specifically exploited.  
3. Missing Recent Literature: Key recent works on efficient transformer adaptation, such as AdaptFormer and related parameter‐efficient fine-tuning methods, are not discussed or compared. This omission weakens the positioning of the proposed method relative to the state of the art.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Clarify the Target Scenario: Provide benchmarks or case studies of devices that truly have high compute but limited storage budgets. Alternatively, adjust the motivating narrative to match more common constraints (e.g., limited memory bandwidth or on‐device storage for parameters).  
2. Emphasize Transformer Specifics: Detail how grouped connections interact with the self‐attention mechanism or feed-forward layers of a transformer. Highlight any design choices that would not apply to, say, convolutional networks or MLP‐based models.  
3. Expand the Literature Review: Incorporate and discuss recent Transformer adaptation techniques (e.g., AdaptFormer, LoRA, Prefix Tuning) and, if possible, provide empirical comparisons to the most relevant of these methods.

Overall, the paper offers an interesting combination of grouping and re-parameterization that achieves measurable efficiency gains. However, the motivation around the deployment scenario is not fully convincing, the claimed transformer-specific contributions are not clearly delineated, and the literature review omits several key recent works. Given these concerns, I find the paper to be marginally below the acceptance threshold (Rating: 5).

title: Equal Improvability: A New Fairness Notion Considering the Long-term Impact

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This work proposes a notion of long-term impact called equal improvability (EI) that equalizes the "effort required to improve" of rejected individuals belonging to different sensitive groups. This metric is theoretically compared to three other long-term metrics: Bounded Effort and Equal Recourse---conditions for meeting each of these notions of fairness (in relation to each other) are derived. Three fairness-based regularization methods are proposed to find policies that mitigate unfairness wrt EI, each with a unique differentiable penalty term. These methods are empirically tested against synthetic and real-world data. In the first set of experiments, the three mitigation strategies are tested against a fairness-unaware algorithm over two real-world datasets (non-dynamic). In the second set of experiments, results for a simple dynamical setting are shown.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths / comments: 
- I enjoyed reading the introduction and the (first) related work section, where the authors pinpoint the importance of their metric and show how it is distinct from current known metrics in the field. 
- Concepts are elaborated on in the appendix or figures are provided to help readers better understand material. The example of $\mu(\Delta \mathbf{x_1})$ in the para underneath Defn 2.1 was very helpful. 
- The authors did not over-state the results of their fairness-based regularization algorithms, e.g., acknowledged that these methods can mitigate EI unfairness and do not provide further guarantees on solutions found. 
- The organization of section 3 was straightforward and well-written. 

Weaknesses / comments: 

- Why don't the authors compare the other long-term fairness metrics to the long-term fairness-unaware metrics in the first set of experiments? ERM is a very simple baseline, and arguably the three datasets provided are three more datasets over which other long-term fairness baselines can be used as a comparison. We could consider this a single-step episode, which is a subset of an n-step episode. The paper that presents ER also presents a fairness-based regularization method to find solutions that mitigate ER. 

- How easy is it to find policies that mitigate unfairness when there are multiple groups, as is the case in many real-world applications? (For example, in applications where one might consider race, gender, or relevant proxies.)  One of the issues with just adding a (fairness-based) penalty to the objective is that the alg is only given incentive to satisfy fairness, but has the freedom to violate fairness if that results in a more improvement to the original objective. Qualitatively, it seems that scaling the importance of each of the additional pair-wise penalties wrt the primary objective would not be simple or insightful for users. 

- Adding on to the previous point, the Zafar paper that inspired the covariance-based EI penalty creates proxies to the original fairness constraint, but does not show (theoretically) that solutions found by the new proxy-objective solve the original objective. The covariance-based EI method also has no guarantees on the solution found by the algorithms (perhaps the KDE-based penalty as well though I am unfamiliar with the work which inspired it, and did not take the time to read through the work). Ultimately this is a weakness of using fairness-based regularization to mitigate unfairness. I suggest that the users address this as a weakness in the main body.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Clarity: Overall the paper is straightforward to read. The proofs I skimmed (app A) seem clear. 
- Page 6 in experimental setting: Where are the "statistics for five trials"? Are the errors standard error or standard deviation? 

Reproducibility: Information needed for reproducibility is located in the appendix. I did not look at the code but the authors also assert that hyperparameters used in the experiments are located there as well. I feel confident that I could re-construct the experiments from the paper.

Quality/Novelty: The authors show that the notion of fairness considered is similar to current long-term metrics but distinct, and provide three algorithms, two of which are small changes from previous works that use soft constraints, i.e., create a fairness-based regularization penalty. These methods do not come with guarantees on the solution found by the optimization process, but can be considered stepping stones for future work.  

Small typos / issues and suggested improvements: 
- Appendix B first sentence: "we explain what each term means in EI definition means" -> "we explain what each term in the EI definition means" 
- I did not realize until the end of the paper that there was a second related work section---I suggest mentioning this in the first related work section! 
- "First-order" is never truly defined, and the reader is left to infer from the text. Does first-order imply that these metrics "take potential follow-up inequity risk" one "time-step" into the future, i.e., one decision into the future? The experimental section shows a one-step scenario (the first set of experiments), and a multi-step scenario (three rounds). Are the long-term metrics mentioned actually n-step fairness notions? 
- EI disparity can be inferred from the main text but I suggest that to reduce confusion it is defined in the main work. ATM the authors refer to appendix B.3 and then use this term later numerous times in the main body.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
I make a recommendation to accept. 

- The approach seems well-motivated, and the authors take care to show that their metric is distinct from other current methods in the longterm fairness space. 
- The paper also does not over-state its claims, e.g., the three optimization algorithms introduced offer no guarantees on the solutions found will actually mitigate EI unfairness. This limitation is well-known in regards to soft constraints but is still important to address in the main body (see weaknesses for an elaboration). 
- There are also a few clarifying points from the experiments I would like addressed, such as the choice of baselines for the non-dynamic experiments, as well as the statistics being presented in the experiments. 

--- post rebuttal --- 
I thank the authors for the detailed response! The authors agreed to address many of my concerns in their next iteration, which I think will make the work more straightforward. After discussion with other reviewers, I still lean towards acceptance.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This work proposes a notion of long-term impact called equal improvability (EI) that equalizes the "effort required to improve" of rejected individuals belonging to different sensitive groups. This metric is theoretically compared to three other long-term metrics: Bounded Effort and Equal Recourse---conditions for meeting each of these notions of fairness (in relation to each other) are derived. Three fairness-based regularization methods are proposed to find policies that mitigate unfairness wrt EI, each with a unique differentiable penalty term. These methods are empirically tested against synthetic and real-world data. In the first set of experiments, the three mitigation strategies are tested against a fairness-unaware algorithm over two real-world datasets (non-dynamic). In the second set of experiments, results for a simple dynamical setting are shown.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strengths / comments: 
- I enjoyed reading the introduction and the (first) related work section, where the authors pinpoint the importance of their metric and show how it is distinct from current known metrics in the field. 
- Concepts are elaborated on in the appendix or figures are provided to help readers better understand material. The example of $\mu(\Delta \mathbf{x_1})$ in the para underneath Defn 2.1 was very helpful. 
- The authors did not over-state the results of their fairness-based regularization algorithms, e.g., acknowledged that these methods can mitigate EI unfairness and do not provide further guarantees on solutions found. 
- The organization of section 3 was straightforward and well-written. 

Weaknesses / comments: 

- Why don't the authors compare the other long-term fairness metrics to the long-term fairness-unaware metrics in the first set of experiments? ERM is a very simple baseline, and arguably the three datasets provided are three more datasets over which other long-term fairness baselines can be used as a comparison. We could consider this a single-step episode, which is a subset of an n-step episode. The paper that presents ER also presents a fairness-based regularization method to find solutions that mitigate ER. 

- How easy is it to find policies that mitigate unfairness when there are multiple groups, as is the case in many real-world applications? (For example, in applications where one might consider race, gender, or relevant proxies.)  One of the issues with just adding a (fairness-based) penalty to the objective is that the alg is only given incentive to satisfy fairness, but has the freedom to violate fairness if that results in a more improvement to the original objective. Qualitatively, it seems that scaling the importance of each of the additional pair-wise penalties wrt the primary objective would not be simple or insightful for users. 

- Adding on to the previous point, the Zafar paper that inspired the covariance-based EI penalty creates proxies to the original fairness constraint, but does not show (theoretically) that solutions found by the new proxy-objective solve the original objective. The covariance-based EI method also has no guarantees on the solution found by the algorithms (perhaps the KDE-based penalty as well though I am unfamiliar with the work which inspired it, and did not take the time to read through the work). Ultimately this is a weakness of using fairness-based regularization to mitigate unfairness. I suggest that the users address this as a weakness in the main body.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Clarity: Overall the paper is straightforward to read. The proofs I skimmed (app A) seem clear. 
- Page 6 in experimental setting: Where are the "statistics for five trials"? Are the errors standard error or standard deviation? 

Reproducibility: Information needed for reproducibility is located in the appendix. I did not look at the code but the authors also assert that hyperparameters used in the experiments are located there as well. I feel confident that I could re-construct the experiments from the paper.

Quality/Novelty: The authors show that the notion of fairness considered is similar to current long-term metrics but distinct, and provide three algorithms, two of which are small changes from previous works that use soft constraints, i.e., create a fairness-based regularization penalty. These methods do not come with guarantees on the solution found by the optimization process, but can be considered stepping stones for future work.  

Small typos / issues and suggested improvements: 
- Appendix B first sentence: "we explain what each term means in EI definition means" -> "we explain what each term in the EI definition means" 
- I did not realize until the end of the paper that there was a second related work section---I suggest mentioning this in the first related work section! 
- "First-order" is never truly defined, and the reader is left to infer from the text. Does first-order imply that these metrics "take potential follow-up inequity risk" one "time-step" into the future, i.e., one decision into the future? The experimental section shows a one-step scenario (the first set of experiments), and a multi-step scenario (three rounds). Are the long-term metrics mentioned actually n-step fairness notions? 
- EI disparity can be inferred from the main text but I suggest that to reduce confusion it is defined in the main work. ATM the authors refer to appendix B.3 and then use this term later numerous times in the main body.

Summary Of The Review:
I make a recommendation to accept. 

- The approach seems well-motivated, and the authors take care to show that their metric is distinct from other current methods in the longterm fairness space. 
- The paper also does not over-state its claims, e.g., the three optimization algorithms introduced offer no guarantees on the solutions found will actually mitigate EI unfairness. This limitation is well-known in regards to soft constraints but is still important to address in the main body (see weaknesses for an elaboration). 
- There are also a few clarifying points from the experiments I would like addressed, such as the choice of baselines for the non-dynamic experiments, as well as the statistics being presented in the experiments.

I thank the authors for the detailed response! The authors agreed to address many of my concerns in their next iteration, which I think will make the work more straightforward. After discussion with other reviewers, I still lean towards acceptance.

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces a novel fairness criterion called Equal Improvability (EI), which measures whether different sensitive groups benefit equally from a classifier in terms of potential score improvements. The authors provide a clear motivation for EI, distinguish it from existing long‐term fairness notions, and develop two regularization strategies (covariance‐based and KDE‐based) to enforce it. The theoretical development is solid, and the organization—particularly Section 3’s exposition of the regularizers—is straightforward. Experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that incorporating EI penalties can reduce disparities across groups, though current empirical comparisons are limited to ERM baselines.

Strengths  
1. Motivation and Novelty: The paper clearly motivates EI as a missing piece in fairness research and positions it distinctly from existing long‐term metrics such as Equal Recourse.  
2. Presentation: The introduction and related‐work sections are well‐written, and the paper makes good use of illustrative examples (e.g., the μ(Δx) figure in the appendix) to build intuition.  
3. Theoretical Caution: The authors are careful not to overclaim, openly acknowledging that their algorithms offer heuristic proxies rather than hard guarantees.  
4. Regularizer Exposition: Section 3 lays out both covariance‐based and KDE‐based penalty designs in a logical, easy‐to‐follow manner, aiding reproducibility.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited Baselines: The experimental comparison is restricted to ERM. Evaluation against other long‐term fairness methods (e.g., Equal Recourse, Balanced Equity) is missing.  
2. Scalability and Multi‐Group Handling: The paper does not discuss how EI regularization extends to more than two sensitive groups, nor how to select or scale penalty weights in that setting.  
3. Lack of Formal Guarantees: Although the covariance‐based and KDE‐based regularizers are well‐motivated proxies, no theoretical proof is provided that they enforce the original EI constraint.  
4. Clarity Issues: Key terms such as “first‐order” and “EI disparity” are only defined in the appendix, and it is unclear whether the reported error bars represent standard deviation or standard error.  
5. Omitted Limitations: The main text does not prominently discuss the gap between the heuristic regularizers and the formal EI criterion, nor does it flag potential drawbacks of the soft‐constraint approach.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Broaden Empirical Comparison: Include at least one long‐term fairness baseline (e.g., Equal Recourse or other single‐step dynamic fairness methods) in the non‐dynamic experiments to better position EI’s contributions.  
2. Address Multi‐Group Scenarios: Add a discussion (and ideally a small experiment) on how the approach scales to more than two sensitive groups, and provide guidance on tuning multiple penalty weights.  
3. Acknowledge Guarantee Gaps: Explicitly state in the main body that the covariance‐based and KDE‐based methods do not come with formal fairness guarantees. This will help readers appreciate the heuristic nature of these proxies.  
4. Clarify Statistical Reporting: In the experimental section, specify whether the error bars correspond to standard deviation or standard error.  
5. Improve Terminology Presentation: Define “first‐order,” “EI disparity,” and related terms in the main text rather than relegating them to the appendix.  
6. Related Work Note: Include a brief remark in the initial related‐work section indicating that a second, focused related‐work discussion appears later.

Decision: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold – Weak Accept)  
Overall, this paper proposes a thoughtfully motivated fairness notion and demonstrates preliminary empirical gains under the new EI metric. While the core idea and exposition are strong, the work would benefit from broader experimental comparisons, clearer statements of methodological limitations, and improved clarity around terminology and reported statistics. With these refinements, EI could make a valuable addition to the fairness literature.

extracted_ideas.summary: The reviewer finds the paper’s new fairness notion (Equal Improvability) well motivated and clearly distinguished from existing metrics, with a solid theoretical foundation and organized regularization methods. They recommend broader experimental comparisons, clearer discussion of limitations (especially around soft‐constraint guarantees and multi‐group settings), and minor clarifications in the text.
strengths: Clear motivation and distinct framing of the new EI metric compared to existing long-term notions
Well-written introduction and related work that position EI’s novelty
Helpful examples and appendix figures (e.g., the μ(Δx) illustration under Definition 2.1)
Authors avoid overstating algorithmic guarantees and acknowledge limitations
Section 3’s organization and exposition of regularization methods is straightforward
weaknesses: First experiments only compare to ERM, not to other long-term fairness baselines (e.g., Equal Recourse)
Unclear how method scales beyond binary sensitive groups and how penalty weights should be tuned
Soft-constraint regularization (covariance-based, KDE-based) lacks theoretical guarantees of satisfying the original fairness criterion
Need to acknowledge regularization guarantee gaps in the main text
Terminology and metrics ("first-order," EI disparity) not defined in the main body
Unclear whether reported error bars are standard deviation or standard error
suggestions: Compare EI mitigation to other long-term fairness baselines (e.g., ER, BE) in the single-step experiments
Discuss applicability and complexity of handling multiple (>2) sensitive groups and penalty weighting
Explicitly note in the main text that regularization methods do not guarantee satisfying original fairness constraints
Clarify in experiments whether error bars represent standard deviation or standard error
Mention in the first related-work section that a second related section appears later
Define terms like “first-order” and “EI disparity” in the main body rather than the appendix
main_points: EI is well motivated and theoretically distinct from existing long-term fairness notions
Regularization methods are clear but lack formal solution guarantees for the intended fairness definitions
Experimental baselines are too narrow—only ERM used in non-dynamic tests
Handling of multiple sensitive groups and penalty scaling is not addressed
Clarity issues around terminology and reported statistics
technical_details: Experiments report "statistics for five trials" but do not specify standard error vs. standard deviation
Hyperparameter settings are provided in the appendix
Covariance-based fairness penalty proxies the original constraint without proof of equivalence
KDE-based penalty is introduced without guarantee it enforces the target fairness metric

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper studies the delayed impact of fairness. The authors introduce a notion of fairness called \emph{equal improvability}, which, subject to a bounded amount of improvement, equalizes the probability of acceptance for the rejected members across all populations. The authors aim to solve the typical loss minimization problem of classification subject to the equal improbability constraint. Since the constraint is non-convex, the paper proposes 3 different approaches to solve the constrained optimization. The paper concludes with empirical analysis both in the one-shot game as well as the repeated setting.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
------------------------------------------
Strengths:
------------------------------------------
-- The delayed impact of fairness in machine learning has not received much attention in the community (mainly due to the hardness of modeling this impact) so in that sense, it is great to see papers on this topic.

--The paper is pretty well written and it is clear to follow the high-level ideas of the paper. The delayed impact notion of fairness is natural and sensible.

------------------------------------------
Weaknesses:
------------------------------------------
-- The main technical contribution of the paper is the definition. Hence, the technical novelty (when enforcing the equal improvability constraint) is limited. 

-- Section 4.2 contains the most interesting part of the paper, though some of the details are hard to follow/justify. It would be great to justify the choice of $\epsilon(x)$ as well as describe in more detail how the updates to the classifier are done.

------------------------------------------
Minor Comments:
------------------------------------------
-- Theorem 2.5 and Corollary 2.6 show that equal improvability and bounded effort are mathematically different. It would be nice to 
describe this in more detail as it is not clear to me how to set the reward function in Heidari et al., 2019 to connect the two works.

-- Are there deeper connections between equal recourse and equal improvability that are overlooked? If not, is not it surprising that these two methods work exactly the same in the experiments in section 4.2?

-- Please stop using the Adult dataset: https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper/2021/hash/32e54441e6382a7fbacbbbaf3c450059-Abstract.html

-- The Y value in Figure 4 should be bounded by 1. Why is it above one in the left two sub-figures?
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: The paper is clear and it is easy to follow the high-level ideas of the paper. The paper is relevant to the community. The definitions are novel though the connections to prior work should be described more clearly (see above). The code is provided.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
Overall, I enjoyed reading the paper. I think it is a nice addition to the growing literature on the delayed impact of fairness. Though the technical contributions are not strong, it can be a welcome addition to the ICLR's program.

------------------------
Post rebuttal:
------------------------
I want to sincerely thank the authors for answering my questions thoroughly. I have also read the other reviews and author responses. I think we all feel that this work can go either way. I am still (slightly) in favor of acceptance though.
