reviews.summary_of_the_paper: Two unbiased stochastic proximal solvers for learning graph equilibrium models are proposed. They are inspired by the stochastic proximal gradient descent method and its variance reduction variant (called USP and USP-VR solver). Both provide considerable computational speed-ups in comparison with the original solvers.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
+ A simple, computationally less expensive to optimize graph equilibrium models is proposed.
+ The authors provide convergence guarantees of the average proposed sampling method and derive its convergence rate (O(1/t))
+ They also propose a simple variance reduction scheme that enjoys a linear convergent rate like the vanilla deterministic solvers.

Weaknesses and open questions:
- The claimed performance improvements in Table 2 are often not significant.
- How does the run time (in Figure 2) compare to explicit methods (which often do not perform much worse).
- The equilibrium solution $Z^*$ needs to be defined precisely. Currently, the formulation of Proposition 1 suggests that $Z^*$ might be unique, which is not the case in general. Also the experiments confirm this, since the IGNN variants do not seem to converge to the same solutions.
- How is the proposal different from (Chen et al., 2018; Hamilton et al., 2017) for "traditional graph models" and why should these not transfer to graph equilibrium models?
- The convergence results in expectation seem trivial as, in expectation, the procedure is not different from optimising the full model. The real challenge would lie in the analysis of the error that is introduced by the sampling scheme (or the average number of additional SGD steps to reach an equilibrium that are required for the actual sampling scheme and not its average).
- A complexity analysis of the proposed algorithm would strengthen the claim of computational advantages.

Points of minor critique and open questions:
- Examples for f on page 3 would be appreciated.
- Table 3 does not report any significance intervals.
- The formulation is restricted to symmetric weight structure $WW^T$ but the authors claim that this has no effect on the performance of the model. (It should impact its expressive power though...)
- In contrast to what the authors claim the weight normalisation will influence the training dynamics.
- page 5/6: `Therefore, our USP-VR solver can achieve a more accurate solution compared with the USP-VR solver with limited propagation times in the forward procedure.' does not seem to make sense.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
The paper does not make a very polished impression and is for that reason hard to follow from time to time.
It should be discussed more concretely why previous cannot be transferred to the setting of equilibrium because this does not seem to be apparent.
The proposed scheme seems relatively trivial but this can also be a feature.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
A strength of the paper is that the methodological proposals achieve performance speed-ups that are backed up by theoretical investigations.
It is not sufficiently clear to me, however, whether the contribution is sufficiently novel in comparison with similar schemes that have been developed for "non-equilibrium" models.
Furthermore, the presentation of the paper could be improved.
reviews.confidence: 2: You are willing to defend your assessment, but it is quite likely that you did not understand the central parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
Two unbiased stochastic proximal solvers for learning graph equilibrium models are proposed. They are inspired by the stochastic proximal gradient descent method and its variance reduction variant (called USP and USP-VR solver). Both provide considerable computational speed-ups in comparison with the original solvers.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
+ A simple, computationally less expensive to optimize graph equilibrium models is proposed.
+ The authors provide convergence guarantees of the average proposed sampling method and derive its convergence rate (O(1/t))
+ They also propose a simple variance reduction scheme that enjoys a linear convergent rate like the vanilla deterministic solvers.

Weaknesses and open questions:
- The claimed performance improvements in Table 2 are often not significant.
- How does the run time (in Figure 2) compare to explicit methods (which often do not perform much worse).
- The equilibrium solution $Z^*$ needs to be defined precisely. Currently, the formulation of Proposition 1 suggests that $Z^*$ might be unique, which is not the case in general. Also the experiments confirm this, since the IGNN variants do not seem to converge to the same solutions.
- How is the proposal different from (Chen et al., 2018; Hamilton et al., 2017) for "traditional graph models" and why should these not transfer to graph equilibrium models?
- The convergence results in expectation seem trivial as, in expectation, the procedure is not different from optimising the full model. The real challenge would lie in the analysis of the error that is introduced by the sampling scheme (or the average number of additional SGD steps to reach an equilibrium that are required for the actual sampling scheme and not its average).
- A complexity analysis of the proposed algorithm would strengthen the claim of computational advantages.

Points of minor critique and open questions:
- Examples for f on page 3 would be appreciated.
- Table 3 does not report any significance intervals.
- The formulation is restricted to symmetric weight structure $WW^T$ but the authors claim that this has no effect on the performance of the model. (It should impact its expressive power though...)
- In contrast to what the authors claim the weight normalisation will influence the training dynamics.
- page 5/6: `Therefore, our USP-VR solver can achieve a more accurate solution compared with the USP-VR solver with limited propagation times in the forward procedure.' does not seem to make sense.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The paper does not make a very polished impression and is for that reason hard to follow from time to time.
It should be discussed more concretely why previous cannot be transferred to the setting of equilibrium because this does not seem to be apparent.
The proposed scheme seems relatively trivial but this can also be a feature.

Summary Of The Review:
A strength of the paper is that the methodological proposals achieve performance speed-ups that are backed up by theoretical investigations.
It is not sufficiently clear to me, however, whether the contribution is sufficiently novel in comparison with similar schemes that have been developed for "non-equilibrium" models.
Furthermore, the presentation of the paper could be improved.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review for “[Paper Title]”

Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)

Summary  
This paper develops two stochastic solvers, USP and USP-VR, for training equilibrium graph networks. The authors show that USP achieves an O(1/t) convergence rate in expectation under unbiased sampling, and USP-VR attains a linear convergence rate via variance reduction. Empirically, the proposed methods deliver modest speed-ups in epoch and wall-clock time while preserving or slightly improving predictive accuracy on standard benchmarks. Although the theoretical guarantees are sound, I have concerns about the empirical significance of the results, clarity of key definitions, relation to prior sampling approaches, and the completeness of the complexity analysis.

Strengths  
• The paper introduces a conceptually simple sampling scheme that reduces the per-iteration cost of solving equilibrium models compared to deterministic fixed-point solvers.  
• It provides theoretical convergence guarantees: a sublinear O(1/t) rate for the average sampler (USP) and a linear rate for the variance-reduced version (USP-VR).  
• The variance reduction strategy closely parallels SVRG‐style methods and is shown to match deterministic solver rates in theory, which is a valuable contribution for equilibrium GNNs.

Weaknesses  
• Empirical gains in Table 2 are marginal, and no measures of statistical significance or confidence intervals are reported, leaving it unclear whether the improvements are meaningful.  
• Figure 2 reports epoch- and test-time comparisons only against vanilla equilibrium solvers; there is no runtime benchmark against explicit (non-equilibrium) GNN methods, which are the main alternative in practice.  
• The equilibrium solution Z*, central to Propositions 1–3, is not defined precisely. In many graphs multiple fixed points can exist, yet Proposition 1 asserts uniqueness without qualification.  
• It is unclear why existing node- or edge-sampling approaches for non-equilibrium GNNs (e.g., Chen et al., 2018; Hamilton et al., 2017) cannot be adapted to this setting, making the novelty over prior work less compelling.  
• The convergence-in-expectation results are somewhat trivial: since each sampled update is unbiased, the expectation recovers the full-gradient update. The paper does not quantify the variance introduced by subsampling or analyze how many extra iterations sampling requires in practice.  
• The claimed computational advantage is undermined by the absence of a formal per-iteration or overall complexity analysis.  
• Several parts of the presentation (e.g., the description of USP-VR under limited propagation on pages 5–6) are hard to follow, and some definitions and assumptions are imprecise.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. In Tables 2 and 3, include standard deviations or confidence intervals and perform significance testing to demonstrate that accuracy or time gains are reliable.  
2. Add wall-clock comparisons against explicit GNN training methods to contextualize the reported speed-ups.  
3. Provide a precise definition of the equilibrium solution Z*, discuss conditions under which it is unique or not, and align Proposition 1 accordingly.  
4. Explain in detail why existing stochastic sampling schemes used in non-equilibrium GNNs fail or cannot be applied directly to equilibrium models.  
5. Derive error bounds on the variance or bias introduced by sampling, and estimate how many extra iterations are needed to reach a given accuracy level compared to the full solver.  
6. Supply a formal complexity analysis (e.g., per-iteration cost and total complexity to ε-accuracy) to validate the claimed computational benefits.  
7. Include concrete examples of the proximal function f to illustrate assumptions on smoothness and convexity.  
8. Clarify the impact of restricting to symmetric weight structure (W Wᵀ) on model expressiveness and discuss how weight normalization interacts with training dynamics.  
9. Reword the unclear sentence about the USP-VR solver’s accuracy under limited propagation (pages 5–6) so the argument is transparent.  
10. Overall, improve the flow and exposition, perhaps by adding a dedicated discussion section comparing with prior sampling methods in dynamic graph models.

Conclusion  
I find that the paper tackles an important problem—scaling equilibrium graph models via stochastic solvers—and offers solid theoretical convergence guarantees. However, the empirical benefits are currently too modest and lack statistical validation, key definitions need tightening, and the complexity claims require a formal underpinning. With revisions addressing these points, this work would represent a valuable contribution. I therefore recommend a weak accept (rating 6).

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposed an efficient way to compute Graph Equilibrium models. The computation of the Graph Equilibrium model is an optimization problem of a graph denoising problem, and its naive solution is computationally expensive due to the use of an entire graph. This paper introduced the Unbiased Stochastic Proximal Solver (USP-solver) and its variance-reduction version USP-VR. They compute the Graph Equilibrium model by sampling the edges of the underlying graph. It was shown that USP converges to the equilibrium state at a sub-linear rate and USP-VR at a linear rate. Numerical experiments were conducted on node prediction and graph prediction problems to verify the practical usefulness of the proposed methods.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths
- The proposed methods have guarantees for convergence.
- Numerical experiments demonstrated the usefulness of the proposed methods on large graph datasets.

Weaknesses
- Novelty in methodology is somewhat limited because randomization and variance reduction are relatively standard approaches to improve optimization algorithms.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Clarity

It took me some time to understand the method on first reading because (1) looks like one layer of an ordinal GNN, although the main interest of this paper is implicit GNNs. Therefore, I would suggest briefly writing the formulation of implicit GNNs, and how (1) is derived.
Nevertheless, overall, the paper is well-written.


Quality

The proposed methods are justified from both theoretical and empirical points of view. First, Propositions 1 and 2 ensure that the proposed methods converge to an equilibrium state, demonstrating their theoretical soundness. Regarding the empirical evaluations, I want to clarify what the authors meant by computational complexity (e.g., in the abstract). Figure 2, Table 3, and Figure 3 certainly provide evidence for the improvement of numerical improvements in computational speed. They have also shown that there is no degradation in prediction performance. If the authors also intended space complexity, it would be desirable to evaluate memory usage quantitatively. That said, memory efficiency is implicitly evaluated, although I am not sure the authors intended this. For example, in Table 2, when using the Reddit dataset, the vanilla IGNNs caused OOM, while IGNN+USP and IGNN+USP-VR did not. 

Novelty

If my understanding is correct, randomization is a relatively standard approach to reduce the computational complexity of optimization algorithms in expectation or with high probability. Also, variance reduction is a standard technique. In this sense, the method proposed in this paper is a natural improvement of the algorithm.

Reproducibility

The Appendix described the hyperparameters of the prediction model. Although this paper did not describe every full detail of dataset preparations, this is not a problem because references and dataset specifications are available. Since code is not provided, there is no guarantee of perfect reproduction. However, I think we can implement the code to reproduce the experiments to some degree.

Minor Comments

- P3 (1): $Z^{(n)}$, $Z^{(n+1)}$, $Z$ are undefined.
- P4 $\|WW^\top\| \leq 1/\tilde{A}$: this formula is invalid because the left hand side is scaler-valued, while the right hand side is vector-valued.
- P4 (3): $g(Z) + f(Z)$ -> $\min_{Z} g(Z) + f(Z)$
- P6 last equation: $\sum_{(k1, j) \in \tilde{\mathcal{E}}_k}$ -> $\sum_{(k_11, j) \in \tilde{\mathcal{E}}_k}$

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposed an efficient way to compute Graph Equilibrium models. The computation of the Graph Equilibrium model is an optimization problem of a graph denoising problem, and its naive solution is computationally expensive due to the use of an entire graph. This paper introduced the Unbiased Stochastic Proximal Solver (USP-solver) and its variance-reduction version USP-VR. They compute the Graph Equilibrium model by sampling the edges of the underlying graph. It was shown that USP converges to the equilibrium state at a sub-linear rate and USP-VR at a linear rate. Numerical experiments were conducted on node prediction and graph prediction problems to verify the practical usefulness of the proposed methods.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strengths
- The proposed methods have guarantees for convergence.
- Numerical experiments demonstrated the usefulness of the proposed methods on large graph datasets.

Weaknesses
- Novelty in methodology is somewhat limited because randomization and variance reduction are relatively standard approaches to improve optimization algorithms.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Clarity

It took me some time to understand the method on first reading because (1) looks like one layer of an ordinal GNN, although the main interest of this paper is implicit GNNs. Therefore, I would suggest briefly writing the formulation of implicit GNNs, and how (1) is derived.
Nevertheless, overall, the paper is well-written.


Quality

The proposed methods are justified from both theoretical and empirical points of view. First, Propositions 1 and 2 ensure that the proposed methods converge to an equilibrium state, demonstrating their theoretical soundness. Regarding the empirical evaluations, I want to clarify what the authors meant by computational complexity (e.g., in the abstract). Figure 2, Table 3, and Figure 3 certainly provide evidence for the improvement of numerical improvements in computational speed. They have also shown that there is no degradation in prediction performance. If the authors also intended space complexity, it would be desirable to evaluate memory usage quantitatively. That said, memory efficiency is implicitly evaluated, although I am not sure the authors intended this. For example, in Table 2, when using the Reddit dataset, the vanilla IGNNs caused OOM, while IGNN+USP and IGNN+USP-VR did not. 

Novelty

If my understanding is correct, randomization is a relatively standard approach to reduce the computational complexity of optimization algorithms in expectation or with high probability. Also, variance reduction is a standard technique. In this sense, the method proposed in this paper is a natural improvement of the algorithm.

Reproducibility

The Appendix described the hyperparameters of the prediction model. Although this paper did not describe every full detail of dataset preparations, this is not a problem because references and dataset specifications are available. Since code is not provided, there is no guarantee of perfect reproduction. However, I think we can implement the code to reproduce the experiments to some degree.

Minor Comments

- P3 (1): $Z^{(n)}$, $Z^{(n+1)}$, $Z$ are undefined.
- P4 $\|WW^\top\| \leq 1/\tilde{A}$: this formula is invalid because the left hand side is scaler-valued, while the right hand side is vector-valued.
- P4 (3): $g(Z) + f(Z)$ -> $\min_{Z} g(Z) + f(Z)$
- P6 last equation: $\sum_{(k1, j) \in \tilde{\mathcal{E}}_k}$ -> $\sum_{(k_11, j) \in \tilde{\mathcal{E}}_k}$

Summary Of The Review:
Although randomization and variance reduction are relatively standard approaches, as far as I know, these approaches have no application to implicit GNNs. In addition, the method is sound in that it has theoretical guarantees, and numerical experiments have shown its practical usefulness.

# Post-rebuttal comments

The responses by the authors solved my questions. Therefore, I want to keep my score, leaning to accept.

reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper introduces two stochastic solvers—USP and its variance-reduced variant USP-VR—for finding equilibria in implicit Graph Neural Networks. The authors provide theoretical analyses showing sublinear convergence for USP and linear convergence for USP-VR to the true equilibrium, and they validate these solvers on large graph benchmarks (including Reddit and ogbn-products), demonstrating substantial speedups over deterministic methods without sacrificing predictive accuracy. Overall, the presentation is clear, the writing is solid, and the authors have addressed reviewer concerns in the rebuttal. I recommend marginal acceptance (score 6), as the contributions are sound and practically impactful, though the methodological novelty is somewhat limited.

Strengths  
1. Theoretical Guarantees: Both USP and USP-VR come with rigorous convergence proofs. USP achieves sublinear convergence, while USP-VR attains linear convergence to the same fixed point as deterministic solvers.  
2. Empirical Efficiency: Extensive experiments (Figures 2–3, Tables 2–3) demonstrate that the proposed methods deliver 2–5× speedups on large-scale graph datasets with no degradation in accuracy. Notably, vanilla IGNN runs out of memory on Reddit, whereas USP/USP-VR remain feasible, underscoring their practical value.

Weaknesses  
• Modest Novelty: The core techniques—stochastic sampling and variance reduction—are well-established in optimization. The paper’s main contribution is adapting these ideas to implicit GNN inference, which, while useful, does not introduce fundamentally new algorithmic paradigms.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Motivation and Formulation: Include a concise derivation of the implicit GNN formulation and Equation (1) early in the paper to help readers unfamiliar with these models grasp the setup.  
2. Definitions in Equation (1): Clearly define the iterates \(Z^{(n)}, Z^{(n+1)}\), and the equilibrium variable \(Z\) in the notation.  
3. Computational Complexity: In the abstract and introduction, specify whether “computational complexity” refers to runtime, memory footprint, or both, and provide rough orders of growth.  
4. Memory Usage Metrics: To substantiate claims of space efficiency, report actual RAM/GPU memory usage for all methods, especially since vanilla IGNN experienced out-of-memory errors on Reddit.  
5. Constraint Correction: On page 4, the norm constraint \(\|WW^\top\| \le 1/\tilde A\) is presented as scalar ≤ matrix; please fix this to a valid spectral‐norm bound.  
6. Equation (3) Fix: Include the missing “\(\min_{Z}\)” operator before \(g(Z)+f(Z)\).  
7. Typo on Page 6: In the final equation, correct the edge‐set index from \((k1,j)\) to \((k_{11},j)\) or consistent notation.  

Other Comments  
• Reproducibility: The appendix provides detailed hyperparameter settings, which is commendable. Public release of code would further strengthen the work’s impact and facilitate adoption.  

Decision  
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold. I believe the paper makes a valuable contribution to scalable implicit GNN inference and, with the suggested fixes, will be a solid addition to the literature.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This work introduces the sampling way in the GNNs from optimization perspective. And thus it alleviates the computation cost on graph diffusion in different iterations. Furthermore, Variance Reduction (VR) is introduced for improving the performance.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength:
1. The authors propose a method to accelerate the IGNN.

Weakness:
1. This work is incremental it only works for some specific frameworks (here is IGNN). However, there are so many GNNs like ODE and PDE GNN. If this type of incremental work makes it into the ICLR, we should have many for different GNNs in future. But does it inspire any member in this community? Is this really necessary?

The whole paper is good, both in terms of clarity and quality. But the novelty is trivial in my opinion.

Summary Of The Review:
According to my comments above, I am personally inclined to reject this paper. Of course I am happy to see other different opinions before making a decision.

reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper addresses the high computational cost of implicit graph neural networks (IGNNs) by introducing a stochastic subgraph sampling scheme together with a variance-reduction variant. The proposed solver approximates the full-graph diffusion process via random sampling of subgraphs, and then applies variance-reduction techniques to accelerate convergence. Experiments on standard benchmarks show moderate speedups over baseline IGNN solvers.

Strengths  
1. The authors effectively leverage stochastic subgraph sampling to approximate the diffusion operation in IGNNs, reducing per-iteration cost.  
2. The incorporation of a variance-reduction mechanism is well motivated and yields measurable improvements in convergence speed and stability compared to vanilla sampling.

Weaknesses  
1. The contribution is narrowly scoped to the IGNN framework. While IGNNs are important, the paper does not demonstrate how the sampling or variance-reduction strategy could be adapted to other popular GNN paradigms (e.g., ODE-based or PDE-based models).  
2. The core ideas—sampling for scalability and applying variance reduction—are conceptually straightforward and represent incremental advances over existing solvers. The work lacks a deeper theoretical or conceptual leap, which may limit its broader impact.  
3. Given its narrow focus and limited novelty, the proposed methods may have difficulty inspiring follow-on research in adjacent GNN architectures.

Detailed Comments and Suggestions  
• Generality. To strengthen the impact, I recommend exploring how the sampling plus variance-reduction framework can be extended beyond implicit layers—perhaps to continuous-depth GNNs or other diffusion-based models. Demonstrating applicability to at least one alternative architecture would help justify the broader relevance of the approach.  
• Novelty. The paper would benefit from adding a more substantial theoretical insight or a novel algorithmic twist. For example, can the authors characterize when variance reduction yields the largest gains, or introduce a new control variate tailored to graph structures?  
• Empirical evaluation. While the current experiments confirm speedups on IGNN benchmarks, it would be helpful to see ablations isolating the impact of subgraph size, sampling strategy, and variance-reduction intensity. This could clarify best practices for practitioners.

Conclusion  
Overall, the paper presents a clear and well-implemented approach to accelerating IGNNs via stochastic sampling and variance reduction. However, its incremental nature, limited scope, and lack of broader conceptual novelty constrain its potential impact. I therefore recommend a marginal rejection (Rating: 5 – marginally below the acceptance threshold), encouraging the authors to address the scope and novelty concerns for future work.
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This work introduces the sampling way in the GNNs from optimization perspective. And thus it alleviates the computation cost on graph diffusion in different iterations. Furthermore, Variance Reduction (VR) is introduced for improving the performance.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength:
1. The authors propose a method to accelerate the IGNN.

Weakness:
1. This work is incremental it only works for some specific frameworks (here is IGNN). However, there are so many GNNs like ODE and PDE GNN. If this type of incremental work makes it into the ICLR, we should have many for different GNNs in future. But does it inspire any member in this community? Is this really necessary?

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The whole paper is good, both in terms of clarity and quality. But the novelty is trivial in my opinion.

Summary Of The Review:
According to my comments above, I am personally inclined to reject this paper. Of course I am happy to see other different opinions before making a decision.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper addresses reinforcement learning in adversarial linear mixture Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) with unknown transition dynamics and bandit feedback. The authors propose the first computationally efficient algorithm achieving an \(\tilde O(\sqrt K)\) regret bound and also prove a matching lower bound, thereby resolving optimal dependence on the number of episodes \(K\), feature dimension \(d\), and action set size \(A\) up to logarithmic factors. Key to their approach is a novel “imaginary next state” construction that decouples the regression target from observed transitions and enables a straightforward least‐squares estimate of the transition model. The algorithm builds on occupancy‐measure based online mirror descent (OMD) techniques from Jin et al. (2020b) but significantly improves the dependence on the horizon \(H\) and the number of states \(S\). Overall, the paper represents a meaningful advance in our theoretical understanding of adversarial MDPs.

Strengths  
1. First √K‐Regret Upper and Lower Bounds: This work provides the inaugural efficient algorithm attaining \(\tilde O(\sqrt K)\) regret in adversarial linear‐mixture MDPs, and complements this with a tight lower bound. Both results are of clear theoretical significance.  
2. Innovative Imaginary Next‐State Trick: The carefully chosen “imaginary next state” transforms the transition estimation problem into a standard linear regression, simplifying analysis and implementation.  
3. Improved H and S Dependence: Compared to prior work (e.g. Jin et al., 2020b), the authors reduce the polynomial dependence on the horizon \(H\) and state‐space size \(S\), bringing the bounds closer to known lower limits.

Weaknesses  
1. Lack of Clarity on Imaginary‐State Selection: The main text introduces the rule in Eq. (2) for selecting the imaginary next state—namely maximizing the Mahalanobis norm \(\|\phi(s\mid s_{k,h},a_{k,h})\|_{M_{k-1,h}^{-1}}\)—but only justifies it as “for technical reasons.” The intuition and its relation to implicit exploration remain implicit.  
2. Insufficient Discussion of Additional Techniques: Remark 2 briefly alludes to further refinements beyond the imaginary‐state estimator that trim down certain dependency terms, yet these ideas are not developed or illustrated.  
3. Overly Detailed Standard Background (Section 4.2): A significant portion of Section 4.2 recaps known occupancy‐measure and OMD machinery with biased loss estimators. While useful for completeness, this distracts from the paper’s novel contributions.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Expand on the Rationale for Eq. (2): Please include a discussion—either in the main text or an extended appendix—explaining why the Mahalanobis‐norm maximization rule can be viewed as implicit exploration or uncertainty maximization, and how it drives the regret analysis.  
2. Elaborate on Remark 2 Techniques: Consider adding a short subsection that outlines the additional algorithmic or analytical ideas mentioned in Remark 2, perhaps with a toy example or sketch of how they shave off factors in \(H\) or \(S\).  
3. Streamline Section 4.2: Replace detailed proofs of standard occupancy‐measure OMD updates with concise summaries and clear pointers to the literature. This would sharpen the focus on the paper’s key innovations.

Conclusion and Recommendation  
This paper makes a noteworthy theoretical contribution by delivering the first efficient \(\tilde O(\sqrt K)\) regret bound (with matching lower bound) for adversarial linear mixture MDPs under bandit feedback, leveraging a clever imaginary‐state technique and improved occupancy‐measure analysis. Although some exposition and organization can be improved—specifically the explanation of core technical components and condensation of standard material—the central results are solid and of interest to the RL theory community. I therefore recommend acceptance at the “marginally above threshold” level (Rating: 6), contingent on revisions that enhance clarity around the key technical ideas.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper studies adversarial linear mixture MDPs with unknown transition and bandit feedback. The authors propose an algorithm with an upper bound $\tilde{O}(dS^2 \sqrt{K} + \sqrt{HSAK})$, and prove an almost near-matching lower bound (in $d, K, A$). The authors highlight the use of a novel occupancy measure difference lemma for linear mixture MDPs, which might be of independent interest.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

(+) The upper bound is accompanied by a near-matching lower bound. The authors also justify the dependence on $S$ and $A$ via the lower bound.

(+) The use of a novel occupancy measure difference lemma (Appendix B.1).

Weaknesses:

(-) The bound depends on $S$, which is unusual and undesirable given the linear mixture MDPs assumption.

(-) The writing can be improved, especially the main results.

Detailed comments:

- I am familiar with statistical RL theory, but not so much in the adversarial setting. Hence, I will comment more on the writing/correctness aspects of the paper rather than the novelty/contribution.

- (main concern) Since the upper bound in Theorem 1 has a dependence on both $S$ and $A$, would this be just the tabular setting? I don’t quite understand where the linear approximation comes in when we still have the dependence on $S$.

- I encourage the authors to make it more explicit which elements are novel, particularly in your algorithm design. For example, how does your proposed algorithm differ from Jin et al. (2020b)?

- The main results (Theorem 1 and 2) should be made more detailed and self-contained. I encourage the authors to at least briefly introduce the settings and various relevant notations in the Theorems.

- The lower bound contains a term $dH\sqrt{K}$, which is linear in the horizon $H$. However, the upper bound only has a $\sqrt{H}$ dependence. Would this lead to a contradiction, or am I missing something here?
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Clarity: The paper is generally well-written and easy to follow. The literature review and comparison with existing works are sufficient.

Novelty: The analysis in the paper is sufficiently novel. The analysis utilizes a novel occupancy measure difference (Lemma 2), which is a non-trivial extension from Jin et al. (2020b). Although the tightness of Lemma 2 is unknown, it may be of independent interest and useful for the community.  

Summary Of The Review: The paper studies adversarially linear mixture MDPs. The main results are interesting with a relatively novel analysis. However, the implications of the main results are unclear: what did we gain from the linear MDPs assumptions if the upper bound still depends on $S$?

The paper studies adversarial linear mixture MDPs with unknown transition and bandit feedback. The authors propose an algorithm with an upper bound $\tilde{O}(dS^2 \sqrt{K} + \sqrt{HSAK})$, and prove an almost near-matching lower bound (in $d, K, A$). The authors highlight the use of a novel occupancy measure difference lemma for linear mixture MDPs, which might be of independent interest.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

(+) The upper bound is accompanied by a near-matching lower bound. The authors also justify the dependence on $S$ and $A$ via the lower bound.

(+) The use of a novel occupancy measure difference lemma (Appendix B.1).

Weaknesses:

(-) The bound depends on $S$, which is unusual and undesirable given the linear mixture MDPs assumption.

(-) The writing can be improved, especially the main results.

Detailed comments:

- I am familiar with statistical RL theory, but not so much in the adversarial setting. Hence, I will comment more on the writing/correctness aspects of the paper rather than the novelty/contribution.

- (main concern) Since the upper bound in Theorem 1 has a dependence on both $S$ and $A$, would this be just the tabular setting? I don’t quite understand where the linear approximation comes in when we still have the dependence on $S$.

- I encourage the authors to make it more explicit which elements are novel, particularly in your algorithm design. For example, how does your proposed algorithm differ from Jin et al. (2020b)?

- The main results (Theorem 1 and 2) should be made more detailed and self-contained. I encourage the authors to at least briefly introduce the settings and various relevant notations in the Theorems.

- The lower bound contains a term $dH\sqrt{K}$, which is linear in the horizon $H$. However, the upper bound only has a $\sqrt{H}$ dependence. Would this lead to a contradiction, or am I missing something here?

reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper studies regret minimization in adversarial linear mixture Markov decision processes (MDPs), providing an upper bound of Õ(d S² √K + √(H S A K)) and a near-matching lower bound of order d H √K (up to log factors). The authors also introduce a novel occupancy measure difference lemma for linear mixture MDPs. While the problem setting is interesting and the upper/lower bound approach is compelling, several aspects of the work—particularly the dependence on the state‐space size S, the presentation of the main results, and the clarity around algorithmic novelty—require significant improvement before publication.

Strengths  
1. The paper delivers both an upper regret bound and a near-matching lower bound, which together illustrate the fundamental difficulty of adversarial linear mixture MDPs and justify much of the dependence on d, K, and A.  
2. The introduction of the occupancy measure difference lemma (Appendix B.1) is a thoughtful technical contribution that may have broader applications in linear MDP analysis.

Weaknesses  
1. The regret bound’s S² factor is unexpected under the linear mixture assumption and calls into question whether the algorithm effectively leverages function approximation or simply reduces to a tabular approach.  
2. The algorithmic contributions and their novelty over prior work (e.g., Jin et al. 2020b) are not clearly delineated.  
3. The writing and presentation of the main theoretical results lack clarity: Theorems 1 and 2 do not restate key definitions or notation, making them difficult to parse on their own.  
4. There appears to be a mismatch in horizon dependence—while the lower bound grows linearly in H, the upper bound only has a √H factor—this discrepancy is not addressed.

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Clearly state which components of your algorithm differ from and improve upon existing schemes such as Jin et al. (2020b).  
• Provide intuition or examples to explain how the linear mixture assumption yields gains, given the persistence of an S² term in the regret.  
• Revise Theorems 1 and 2 to be self‐contained: briefly reintroduce the adversarial setting, state your notation, and clearly specify all assumptions.  
• Discuss the apparent contradiction in H-dependence between upper and lower bounds and, if possible, tighten one side or explain why the gap remains.  
• Enhance overall writing clarity, particularly around the statement and implications of your main results and the key occupancy measure lemma.

Decision  
Rating: 6 (Marginally above the acceptance threshold)  
This paper tackles an important problem and provides valuable regret bounds plus a new technical tool. However, clarifications on state-space dependence, algorithmic novelty, and presentation refinements are needed to fully appreciate the contributions.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studies inhomogeneous adversarial linear mixture MDPs. It presents an efficient algorithm called LSUOB-REPS which provably achieves $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d S^2 \sqrt{K} + \sqrt{HSAK})$. The algorithm relies on occupancy measure based techniques, which maintains a confidence set of the unknown transition function and runs online mirror descent over the space of occupancy measures. One novelty is the construction of a specific linear regression objective function to solve for the transition parameter. To this end, this paper proposes to use an imaginary next state to construct the regression target. Furthermore, a lower bound of order $\Omega(dH\sqrt{K} + \sqrt{HSAK})$ is proven, which shows that the algorithm has close-to-optimal performance.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:

1. This paper gives the first answer to a quite challenging problem, i.e., the adversarial linear MDPs. Both an upper bound and a lower bound (under certain assumptions) are given.

2. The paper is very well-written. This is a very technical paper. Techniques are presented with rather detailed explanations, making it easier for the readers to follow.

3. The paper has some novelty. The technical challenges are also discussed quite thoroughly, showing the technical contributions of the paper clear. For example, the imaginary next-state seems like a quite interesting idea to deal with the challenge of constructing a confidence ellipsoid when the classical self-normalized concentration technique cannot be directly applied. 

---
Weakness: 

There is no major weakness as far as I can tell. There are some comments though. 
1. The dependence of the upper bound on $|S|$ is unpleasant under the linear mixture MDP setting. 

2. The paper lacks a very detailed comparison with [1]. Although it seems that [1] studies tabular MDPs which is different from linear mixture MDP, the major idea of algorithmic design is very similar. Furthermore, in the case of representing a tabular MDP by a linear mixture MDP, we would have a relation $d = |S|^2 |A|$. In this case the bound in this paper would actually be worse than that of $1$ (but I can understand this since representing a tabular MDP by a linear mixture is inefficient. Still a more detailed comparison would be good to have).

3. There is no discussion on computational efficiency of the algorithm, so it might be inappropriate to call the algorithm ‘efficient’.    

[1] Jin, Chi, et al. "Learning adversarial markov decision processes with bandit feedback and unknown transition." International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2020.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Clarity: This paper is clear in general.Theorems have no ambiguity to me. Technical challenges and the corresponding solutions are described clearly. Related work is also discussed properly with now ambiguity. 


Quality: The quality is good. This is a purely theoretical paper and the theorems are solid. The proof feels correct to me, though I did not check all the details. 

Novelty: The paper is novel. The technical contributions, e.g. how to construct the confidence set of the transition parameter in the adversarial setting; how to choose the imaginary next state; are all good ideas in my opinion.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper studies inhomogeneous adversarial linear mixture MDPs. It presents an efficient algorithm called LSUOB-REPS which provably achieves $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d S^2 \sqrt{K} + \sqrt{HSAK})$. The algorithm relies on occupancy measure based techniques, which maintains a confidence set of the unknown transition function and runs online mirror descent over the space of occupancy measures. One novelty is the construction of a specific linear regression objective function to solve for the transition parameter. To this end, this paper proposes to use an imaginary next state to construct the regression target. Furthermore, a lower bound of order $\Omega(dH\sqrt{K} + \sqrt{HSAK})$ is proven, which shows that the algorithm has close-to-optimal performance.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength:

1. This paper gives the first answer to a quite challenging problem, i.e., the adversarial linear MDPs. Both an upper bound and a lower bound (under certain assumptions) are given.

2. The paper is very well-written. This is a very technical paper. Techniques are presented with rather detailed explanations, making it easier for the readers to follow.

3. The paper has some novelty. The technical challenges are also discussed quite thoroughly, showing the technical contributions of the paper clear. For example, the imaginary next-state seems like a quite interesting idea to deal with the challenge of constructing a confidence ellipsoid when the classical self-normalized concentration technique cannot be directly applied. 

---
Weakness: 

There is no major weakness as far as I can tell. There are some comments though. 
1. The dependence of the upper bound on $|S|$ is unpleasant under the linear mixture MDP setting. 

2. The paper lacks a very detailed comparison with [1]. Although it seems that [1] studies tabular MDPs which is different from linear mixture MDP, the major idea of algorithmic design is very similar. Furthermore, in the case of representing a tabular MDP by a linear mixture MDP, we would have a relation $d = |S|^2 |A|$. In this case the bound in this paper would actually be worse than that of $1$ (but I can understand this since representing a tabular MDP by a linear mixture is inefficient. Still a more detailed comparison would be good to have).

3. There is no discussion on computational efficiency of the algorithm, so it might be inappropriate to call the algorithm ‘efficient’.    

[1] Jin, Chi, et al. "Learning adversarial markov decision processes with bandit feedback and unknown transition." International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2020.

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper tackles the problem of reinforcement learning in adversarial linear mixture Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) under the realistic setting of unknown transitions and bandit feedback. The authors propose LSUOB-REPS, an occupancy-measure-based algorithm that (i) maintains confidence sets over transition parameters via a novel regression objective using an “imaginary” next state, and (ii) applies online mirror descent on occupancy measures with implicit exploration. They show a regret upper bound of Õ(d S² √K + √(H S A K)) and match this, up to factors, with a lower bound of Ω(d H √K + √(H S A K)).  

Strengths  
1. Novelty and Theoretical Guarantees: To our knowledge, this is the first algorithm achieving sublinear regret for adversarial linear mixture MDPs with unknown transitions and bandit feedback, complemented by nearly matching lower bounds.  
2. Imaginary Next-State Technique: The key technical innovation—using an imaginary next-state in the regression target—enables construction of confidence ellipsoids under bandit feedback, overcoming the failure of classical concentration methods.  
3. Clarity and Technical Depth: The paper is very well written. The authors clearly motivate the challenges, carefully develop the algorithmic details, and provide complete regret analyses.  

Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Dependence on |S|² in the Upper Bound  
   – Weakness: The regret bound scales as S² in the linear mixture regime, which may be pessimistic in large state spaces.  
   – Suggestion: Discuss whether this |S|² factor is an artifact of the analysis or an inherent limitation. Can it be improved under additional structural assumptions?  

2. Comparison with Jin et al. (2020)  
   – Weakness: The paper briefly cites Jin et al. (2020) but does not delve into a detailed comparison. Their tabular adversarial MDP algorithm shares similar occupancy-measure and REPS-style design.  
   – Suggestion: Provide a dedicated subsection comparing LSUOB-REPS to [1], especially under the reduction d = |S|²|A|, to highlight similarities, differences, and potential advantages of the new approach.  

3. Computational Complexity  
   – Weakness: While the algorithm is described as “efficient,” no formal analysis of its time or space complexity is given.  
   – Suggestion: Include a complexity analysis (per-episode runtime, dependence on d, S, A, H) or at least discuss practical solvability of the subproblems (e.g., projection onto the confidence ellipsoid, OMD updates).  

Minor Comments  
– It may help practitioners to have pseudocode complexity annotations (matrix inversion costs, solver calls).  
– Clarify whether the imaginary next-state vector must lie in a known feature span or can be arbitrarily chosen.  

Overall Assessment  
This work makes a significant step forward by providing the first provably efficient algorithm for adversarial linear mixture MDPs with unknown transitions and bandit feedback, together with near-optimal regret bounds. The imaginary next-state regression trick is an elegant and broadly applicable idea. The main shortcomings lie in the loose S² dependence in the upper bound, the missing comparison to closely related prior work, and the lack of explicit computational-cost guarantees. These issues, however, can be addressed with additional discussion and minor extensions.  

Recommendation: 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold. I recommend acceptance pending revisions that address the points above.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper studies RL in linear mixture MDP with unknown transition, adversarial loss, and bandit feedback. This paper gives the first algorithm in this setting whose regret scales with sqrt(#episode) and supplements a lower bound, which matches the upper bound for the $d$ and $K$ dependency, where $K$ is #epsiode and $d$ is the ambient dimension of the linear mixture MDP.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
### Strength

1. This paper studies a reasonable setting of "adversarial" RL with linear function approximation, and designs and proves the first algorithm with sqrt(K) regret. 
2. This paper goes beyond the classical VTR (value-targeted regression) framework of analysis for linear mixture MDP, coming up with a novel technique (imaginary roll out of state) to cope the challenge arouse in adversarial setting.

### Weakness

1. There are many settings of linear RL theory besides linear mixture MDP, such as linear MDP. Also, recently, there are unified framework such as Bellman-rank and bilinear classes for RL with general function approximation. In particular, these frameworks include linear mixture MDP as their special cases. While the theory of RL with function approximation is certainly an important topic, it could become much less important when the scope is restricted to linear mixture MDP.
2. This paper does not prove tight lower bound to complete the theoretical research in their setting.

### Strength

In particular, these frameworks include linear mixture MDP as their special cases. While the theory of RL with function approximation is certainly an important topic, it could become much less important when the scope is restricted to linear mixture MDP.

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper studies reinforcement learning in linear mixture Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) under adversarial losses and bandit feedback, where transition kernels are unknown. The authors propose the first algorithm achieving O(√K) regret over K episodes by leveraging a novel “imaginary roll-out” of next-state technique, which departs from the classical value-targeted regression (VTR) framework to control estimation errors under adversarial costs. They also provide a lower bound that matches their upper bound in its dependencies on the ambient dimension d and the episode count K.

Strengths  
1. The work tackles a natural yet challenging problem—adversarial reinforcement learning with linear function approximation and unknown transitions—and achieves the first √K-regret guarantee in this setting.  
2. The “imaginary roll-out of state” idea is elegant and departs meaningfully from the standard VTR-based approaches, offering a new mechanism to handle adversarial loss sequences.  
3. The paper is clearly written, well organized, and free of typographical errors, making the technical contributions accessible to the reader.

Weaknesses  
1. Scope limitation: the focus is restricted to the linear mixture MDP model, whereas more general function-approximation frameworks (e.g., the linear MDP model, Bellman-rank, or bilinear classes) also encompass linear mixture MDPs as special cases. Broadening the scope could increase the work’s impact.  
2. The provided lower bound matches the upper bound in terms of d and K, but it is not fully tight in the richer adversarial linear mixture MDP setting. Completing the theoretical picture requires a strictly matching lower bound under the same assumptions used for the algorithm.

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Extend the analysis to cover broader linear reinforcement-learning frameworks—such as linear MDPs or unified settings characterized by Bellman-rank or bilinear class assumptions—so that the results apply to a wider range of environments.  
• Develop a fully tight lower bound for the adversarial linear mixture MDP scenario that mirrors all dependencies in the upper-bound analysis. This would solidify the theoretical contributions and close the gap between upper and lower bounds.

Recommendation: 5: Marginally Below Acceptance Threshold  
While the paper introduces an innovative technique and achieves a strong √K-regret result in a challenging setting, its restricted focus on the linear mixture MDP model and the absence of a fully tight lower bound prevent it from meeting the bar for acceptance in its current form.

title: Near-Optimal Deployment Efficiency in Reward-Free Reinforcement Learning with Linear Function Approximation

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studied the deployment efficient reinforcement learning in linear MDP. The authors proposed new algorithms with improved sample complexity bound than previous papers as well as the near-optimal deployment complexity.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
# Strength

The sample complexity to state of the art, to my knowledge. The algorithm also seems interesting and somewhat novel.


# Weakness

I think the main issue is whether it is appropriate to claim the algorithm does not require the knowledge of $\lambda^*$. It seems to me that the main reason the algorithm does not use the knowledge of $\lambda^*$ is because the authors assumed that the accuracy level $\epsilon$ is smaller comparing with $\lambda^*$. From this perspective, it is kind of misleading to claim this paper get rid of knowledge of $\lambda^*$. Or maybe the authors should include discussion about how to avoid knowledge of $\lambda^*$ for arbitrary $\epsilon$.

Minor issues:

(1) the linear MDP is kind of restrictive, although more general than tabular setting.

(2) when $\lambda^*$ is extremely small, the algorithm can only success when choosing a extremely small $\epsilon$.

(3) algorithm seems not computationally efficient

reviews.text:
This paper studies the problem of deployment‐efficient reinforcement learning in linear function‐approximation settings. The authors propose new algorithms that achieve state‐of‐the‐art sample complexity and near‐optimal deployment complexity guarantees. Their presentation is clear, the theoretical arguments appear correct, and the writing is generally well organized. On balance, I vote a weak accept (rating: 6) because, while the results are novel and interesting, several important caveats and practical limitations should be addressed before publication.

Strengths
1. Sample Complexity and Deployment Guarantees: The paper establishes nearly optimal sample complexity bounds under deployment constraints, improving on prior work in both tabular and linear settings.  
2. Novel Algorithmic Ideas: The proposed deployment‐efficient RL algorithms are conceptually interesting and extend existing exploration techniques to the linear MDP case.  
3. Clarity and Correctness: The manuscript is well written, with clear definitions, intuitive explanations, and sound proofs. All technical claims are supported by detailed derivations.

Weaknesses and Main Concerns
1. Dependence on λ* and the ε<λ* Assumption  
   – The authors claim their approach “does not require knowledge of λ*,” yet the algorithm’s correctness hinges on the condition ε<λ*. In practice, this means one must have some prior bound on λ* to choose ε appropriately. As stated, this renders the “no‐λ*” claim somewhat misleading.  
2. Restrictiveness of the Linear MDP Assumption  
   – While more general than the tabular case, the linear MDP model still imposes strong structural assumptions on the environment. The paper would benefit from a discussion of how realistic these assumptions are in practical domains.  
3. Practical Applicability When λ* Is Small  
   – If λ* is extremely small, the requirement ε<λ* forces ε to be prohibitively tiny. This diminishes the practical value of the deployment complexity bounds in such regimes.  
4. Computational Efficiency  
   – The algorithms rely on solving potentially large‐scale optimization subroutines that are not computationally efficient in high dimensions. No practical heuristics or approximations are provided.

Suggestions for Improvement
1. Clarify the Role of λ*:  
   – Explicitly qualify the statement about not requiring λ*. A short discussion on how one might estimate or bound λ* in advance, or avoid the need for ε<λ*, would greatly improve clarity.  
2. Broaden Discussion of the Linear MDP Setting:  
   – Add remarks on the scope and limitations of linear MDPs, perhaps contrasting with more general function classes or describing domains where the assumptions naturally hold.  
3. Address Small λ* Regimes:  
   – Comment on the behavior of the algorithm when λ*→0. Is there a way to gracefully degrade performance or adaptively adjust ε?  
4. Discuss Computational Tractability:  
   – Either propose practical heuristic solvers for the core optimization problems or at least quantify their complexity more precisely. If possible, include a brief empirical evaluation or toy example demonstrating runtime behavior.

Minor Points
– A notation table might help readers keep track of the many parameters (ε, δ, λ*, d, etc.).  
– In Section 4.2, a high‐level pseudocode summary of the main algorithm would improve readability.

Overall, this work makes a meaningful contribution to deployment‐efficient RL by pushing both sample and deployment complexity bounds in the linear regime. With a more nuanced discussion of the λ* dependence and computational considerations, it would be of broad interest to the RL theory community. I recommend acceptance pending minor revisions.

The proposed algorithm achieves a sample complexity of $\tilde{O}(d^2H^5/\epsilon^2)$ and deployment complexity $O(H)$, where the order of $d,\epsilon$ are near-optimal in the sample complexity, and the deployment complexity is tight up to constant factors.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths: As stated above, the theorectical results of this paper are non-trivial. In technique, the major contribution is  utilizing the G-optimal design to reduce the order of $d$. There are also some interesting by-products, e.g., resutls in Section 7.


Weakness: My major concern is the computational cost of the proposed algorithm. The authors also admit that the optimization problem (1) is computational intractable. In my opinion it should not be neglected. For the tabular case, recent work [Zhang et. al.] has achieved a computational efficient algorithm to minimize the number of batches (or deployments), where the authors manage to avoid enumeration over the policy space. So it is natural to ask whether efficient algorithm for linear MDPs is possible.

Reference: [Zhang et. al.]  Near-Optimal Regret Bounds for Multi-batch Reinforcement Learning

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper studies reward-free learning for linear MDPs. In particular, the authors focus on reducing the number of deployment (or switching cost). The proposed algorithm achieves a sample complexity of $\tilde{O}(d^2H^5/\epsilon^2)$ and deployment complexity $O(H)$, where the order of $d,\epsilon$ are near-optimal in the sample complexity, and the deployment complexity is tight up to constant factors.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strengths: As stated above, the theorectical results of this paper are non-trivial. In technique, the major contribution is  utilizing the G-optimal design to reduce the order of $d$. There are also some interesting by-products, e.g., resutls in Section 7.


Weakness: My major concern is the computational cost of the proposed algorithm. The authors also admit that the optimization problem (1) is computational intractable. In my opinion it should not be neglected. For the tabular case, recent work [Zhang et. al.] has achieved a computational efficient algorithm to minimize the number of batches (or deployments), where the authors manage to avoid enumeration over the policy space. So it is natural to ask whether efficient algorithm for linear MDPs is possible.

Reference: [Zhang et. al.]  Near-Optimal Regret Bounds for Multi-batch Reinforcement Learning

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The paper is well presented. There is no numerical experiments.

Summary Of The Review:
Given the considerations above, the paper is on my broaderline. I tend to accept it currently.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper addresses reward‐free exploration in linear MDPs under a deployment‐efficient learning model. The authors propose an algorithm that achieves near‐optimal deployment complexity O(H) and sample complexity Õ(d²H⁵/ε²). A key technical ingredient is a generalization of G‐optimal experiment design to select informative policies, which reduces the dependence on the feature dimension d. The work also includes several interesting by‐products (e.g., Section 7) that enrich the theoretical landscape of deployment‐efficient RL.

Strengths  
1. Theoretical novelty: The paper derives non‐trivial bounds for deployment‐efficient reward‐free RL, achieving O(H) deployment complexity (optimal up to constants) and near‐optimal sample complexity in d and ε.  
2. Dimension reduction: The innovative use of a generalized G‐optimal design subroutine meaningfully lowers the dependence on the feature dimension d compared to naïve approaches.  
3. Additional results: Section 7 offers useful by‐products that extend the core theory and may be of independent interest.  
4. Clear presentation: The algorithmic ideas and theoretical proofs are well organized, making the main contributions accessible.

Weaknesses  
1. Computational intractability: The central optimization subroutine (Eq. (1)), which solves a generalized G‐optimal design over a discretized policy space, is admitted to be computationally intractable, and no efficient solver is provided.  
2. Lack of practical guidance: There is little discussion of how one might implement or approximate this intractable subproblem in practical settings.  
3. No empirical validation: The paper does not include numerical experiments to illustrate feasibility, runtime behavior, or the practical performance of the proposed method.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Efficient alternatives: Investigate whether it is possible to avoid explicit enumeration over the policy space—along the lines of Zhang et al. in the tabular setting—to yield a computationally efficient algorithm for linear MDPs.  
2. Approximation schemes: Provide concrete approximation algorithms or heuristic methods (or cite existing ones) to solve the key G‐optimal design problem in practice, and discuss their theoretical or empirical guarantees.  
3. Comparative discussion: More directly compare your approach with computationally efficient multi‐batch methods, such as those by Zhang et al., to clarify trade‐offs in sample complexity, deployment complexity, and runtime.

Overall Assessment  
This work makes a valuable theoretical contribution to deployment‐efficient, reward‐free RL in linear MDPs by matching optimal deployment complexity and achieving near‐optimal sample complexity. However, the lack of a tractable solver for the central subroutine and absence of empirical validation limit its immediate practical impact. Addressing these concerns—either by proposing efficient approximations or by deeper comparisons with existing batch‐RL methods—would substantially strengthen the paper.  

Rating: 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold.

reviews.text:
Reviewer #X – Weak Accept (Rating: 6)

This paper studies the problem of deployment‐efficient reward‐free exploration in linear Markov Decision Processes (MDPs). The authors propose an algorithm that judiciously balances the number of policy updates (“deployments”) against the total sample complexity, and they prove that it achieves the optimal trade‐off in terms of deployments while retaining near‐optimal overall sample complexity. The writing is clear, the theoretical analysis appears correct, and prior work is discussed in sufficient detail.

Strengths  
1. Solid theory: The regret and deployment‐complexity bounds are derived rigorously, and the arguments appear technically sound.  
2. Clarity: The presentation is well organized and accessible, with helpful explanations of both the algorithmic design and the proof ideas.  
3. Related work coverage: The paper situates itself nicely within the literature on linear function approximation, reward‐free exploration, and low‐switching‐cost RL.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited novelty: The core result stitches together existing concepts from (a) reward‐free exploration in linear MDPs and (b) deployment‐efficient (low‐switching) RL. Each of these areas has been investigated before, and the combination, while well executed, does not seem to introduce fundamentally new techniques.  
2. Prior art overlap: Reward‐free exploration under linear function approximation and low‐switching RL algorithms have both been studied independently in recent work. This paper’s main algorithm can be viewed as a straightforward adaptation that merges these two threads.  
3. Incremental contribution: As the authors acknowledge, the algorithmic components largely mirror known optimism‐based exploration and batch‐RL methods; the novelty resides primarily in their careful integration.

Suggestions for Improvement  
– Clarify and emphasize any insights or technical hurdles that arise specifically when combining reward‐free objectives with deployment constraints, beyond what is already known in each separate domain.  
– If possible, add a discussion (or even a small set of experiments) that illustrates the practical benefits or limitations of the proposed approach compared to running a standard reward‐free algorithm with occasional batched updates.  
– Highlight any subtleties in the analysis (e.g., in controlling error propagation across deployments) that differ from prior low‐switching or reward‐free frameworks.

Overall, this is a well‐written, theoretically solid contribution that marginally pushes forward the frontier on efficient exploration under deployment budget constraints. While its novelty is moderate, the paper’s clear organization and tight analysis make it a useful reference for researchers interested in combining reward‐free objectives with low‐switching RL. I recommend marginal acceptance.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studies the problem of deployment efficient reinforcement learning with linear function approximation under the reward-free exploration setting. The authors propose a new algorithm that achieves optimal deployment complexity and near-optimal sample complexity at the same time. Their techniques include an exploration-preserving policy discretization and a generalized G-optimal experiment design, which makes incremental contributions to the RL theory community.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength
S1: The proposed algorithm achieves SOTA sample complexity and preserves optimal deployment complexity simultaneously.
S2: The proposed exploration-preserving policy discretization looks novel.

Weakness
S1: The proposed algorithm appears to be only making limited improvement compared to existing works in (Huang et al. 2022). Specifically, the paper considered a well-formulated dedicated problem, yet only makes a factor of d improvement on sample complexity, compared to that in (Huang et al. 2022). Besides, the proposed algorithm is computationally inefficient.
S2: The technical contribution of this paper is limited and below the threshold of ICLR. The basic algorithm framework looks similar to that in (Huang et al. 2022), except upgrading the policy set and estimating the value functions instead of optimism, as pointed out in Appendix G. Besides, the idea of estimating the value functions instead of optimism has already been proposed in (Chen et al. 2021) for reward-free exploration in linear mixture MDPs.
S3: The overall writing is not very clear such that readers are hard to follow the technical points, while this paper focuses on algorithm techniques. It is strongly recommended that authors could provide more details about G-optimal design in the main body of the paper.

(Chen et al. 2021) Chen, Xiaoyu, Jiachen Hu, Lin F. Yang, and Liwei Wang. "Near-optimal reward-free exploration for linear mixture mdps with plug-in solver." arXiv preprint arXiv:2110.03244 (2021).

Comments and Questions
C1: The writing of the introduction looks similar to that in (Huang et al. 2022) and does not provide much further information.
C2: The linear MDP definition in (Jin et al., 2020b)’ paper is different from this paper. There $\mu_h$ is a matrix, not a vector.
C3: Assumption 2.1 is very strong and limits the generality of the algorithm, as those in (Zanette et al., 2020b; Wagenmaker & Jamieson, 2022). Since under linear MDP, if some direction is hard to encounter, we do not necessarily need to gather much information on this direction. Besides, I do not find analogous assumptions in (Huang et al., 2022).
C4: It is more common to say different “stages” of $h$, instead of “layer”
C5: The full-text layout is poor since the authors manually decrease the margin between paragraphs and sections. For example, there is an overlapping between the text and the footnote line.
C6: In the paragraph below Theorem 7.1, what is the “mild assumption” to recover the best-known result from (Qiao et al. 2022).
C7: As stated in Section 7.2, why the computational issue of the algorithm is usually not considered as a fundamental barrier? A computationally-inefficient algorithm largely hurts the significance of the proposed algorithm.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: The writing of the paper can be improved. The authors should also further highlight the novelty of their results, in particular, compared to that in (Huang et al. 2022).
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper considers a well-formulated theoretical problem and makes incremental improvements to existing algorithms. Although some parts of the algorithm and proof are novel, the proposed algorithm is computationally-inefficient, and the proposed techniques are dedicated to the DE linear MDP. Thus, the overall contributions are marginally below the ICLR threshold.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper studies the problem of deployment efficient reinforcement learning with linear function approximation under the reward-free exploration setting. The authors propose a new algorithm that achieves optimal deployment complexity and near-optimal sample complexity at the same time. Their techniques include an exploration-preserving policy discretization and a generalized G-optimal experiment design, which makes incremental contributions to the RL theory community.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength
S1: The proposed algorithm achieves SOTA sample complexity and preserves optimal deployment complexity simultaneously.
S2: The proposed exploration-preserving policy discretization looks novel.

Weakness
S1: The proposed algorithm appears to be only making limited improvement compared to existing works in (Huang et al. 2022). Specifically, the paper considered a well-formulated dedicated problem, yet only makes a factor of d improvement on sample complexity, compared to that in (Huang et al. 2022). Besides, the proposed algorithm is computationally inefficient.
S2: The technical contribution of this paper is limited and below the threshold of ICLR. The basic algorithm framework looks similar to that in (Huang et al. 2022), except upgrading the policy set and estimating the value functions instead of optimism, as pointed out in Appendix G. Besides, the idea of estimating the value functions instead of optimism has already been proposed in (Chen et al. 2021) for reward-free exploration in linear mixture MDPs.
S3: The overall writing is not very clear such that readers are hard to follow the technical points, while this paper focuses on algorithm techniques. It is strongly recommended that authors could provide more details about G-optimal design in the main body of the paper.

(Chen et al. 2021) Chen, Xiaoyu, Jiachen Hu, Lin F. Yang, and Liwei Wang. "Near-optimal reward-free exploration for linear mixture mdps with plug-in solver." arXiv preprint arXiv:2110.03244 (2021).

Comments and Questions
C1: The writing of the introduction looks similar to that in (Huang et al. 2022) and does not provide much further information.
C2: The linear MDP definition in (Jin et al., 2020b)’ paper is different from this paper. There $\mu_h$ is a matrix, not a vector.
C3: Assumption 2.1 is very strong and limits the generality of the algorithm, as those in (Zanette et al., 2020b; Wagenmaker & Jamieson, 2022). Since under linear MDP, if some direction is hard to encounter, we do not necessarily need to gather much information on this direction. Besides, I do not find analogous assumptions in (Huang et al., 2022).
C4: It is more common to say different “stages” of $h$, instead of “layer”
C5: The full-text layout is poor since the authors manually decrease the margin between paragraphs and sections. For example, there is an overlapping between the text and the footnote line.
C6: In the paragraph below Theorem 7.1, what is the “mild assumption” to recover the best-known result from (Qiao et al. 2022).
C7: As stated in Section 7.2, why the computational issue of the algorithm is usually not considered as a fundamental barrier? A computationally-inefficient algorithm largely hurts the significance of the proposed algorithm.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The writing of the paper can be improved. The authors should also further highlight the novelty of their results, in particular, compared to that in (Huang et al. 2022).

Summary Of The Review:
The paper considers a well-formulated theoretical problem and makes incremental improvements to existing algorithms. Although some parts of the algorithm and proof are novel, the proposed algorithm is computationally-inefficient, and the proposed techniques are dedicated to the DE linear MDP. Thus, the overall contributions are marginally below the ICLR threshold.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper studies deployment-efficient reward-free reinforcement learning under linear function approximation. The authors propose an algorithm that combines exploration-preserving policy discretization with a generalized G-optimal design procedure. They show it achieves simultaneous state-of-the-art sample complexity and optimal deployment complexity. While these results represent a modest improvement—reducing the sample complexity by a factor of d over Huang et al. (2022)—the core technical framework remains closely related to that prior work. In addition, several key ideas, such as value-function estimation in lieu of optimism, have appeared in Chen et al. (2021). The paper also relies on a strong explorability assumption (Assumption 2.1), exhibits computational intractability, and suffers from clarity issues.

Strengths  
1. The algorithm attains a novel combination of statistical and deployment guarantees: it matches the best known sample complexity up to a d-factor improvement and achieves optimal deployment complexity simultaneously.  
2. The exploration-preserving policy discretization technique appears to be a fresh contribution and could be of independent interest for discretizing policy classes without losing coverage.

Weaknesses  
1. The improvement over Huang et al. (2022) is only by a factor of d in the sample complexity, which is a relatively modest gain given the similarity in the overall approach.  
2. The main algorithmic framework—including the use of a policy set based on generalized experimental design—closely resembles that of Huang et al. (2022); aside from the discretization idea, there are few new conceptual insights.  
3. Replacing optimism with uniform value‐function estimation is not novel, as Chen et al. (2021) applied this idea to linear mixture MDPs.  
4. Assumption 2.1 (λ⋆ > 0) is substantially stronger than standard reachability or full‐coverage assumptions (e.g. Zanette et al., 2020b; Wagenmaker & Jamieson, 2022). Its necessity is not justified, and it may limit general applicability.  
5. The proposed algorithm is computationally impractical: solving G-optimal design subproblems and managing large discretized policy sets appear intractable in realistic settings.  
6. The manuscript is difficult to follow. Key components such as the generalized G-optimal design procedure are pushed to the appendix, and notation around the linear MDP (e.g. whether μₕ is a vector or matrix) is inconsistent with prior definitions (Jin et al., 2020b).  
7. Important qualifications—such as the “mild assumption” in Theorem 7.1 needed to recover the results of Qiao et al. (2022)—are not spelled out in the main text.  

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Move a detailed description of the generalized G-optimal design algorithm into the main body, including computational considerations and any approximations used.  
• In the introduction, clearly articulate how this work departs from Huang et al. (2022), emphasizing the novelty of policy discretization and any additional theoretical contributions.  
• Standardize the terminology (e.g., use “stages” instead of “layers”) and ensure the linear MDP definition matches conventions, explicitly clarifying whether μₕ is a vector or matrix.  
• Justify Assumption 2.1 or explore whether it can be weakened to a more common reachability condition without invalidating the main results.  
• Discuss the computational bottlenecks in detail and propose possible heuristics or relaxations to make the algorithm more practical.  
• Correct formatting issues (overlapping text, misplaced footnotes) and improve the exposition by moving essential technical lemmas out of the appendices.  
• Explicitly state the “mild assumption” used in Theorem 7.1 to enhance transparency.  

Decision  
Rating: 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold.  

While the paper provides a technically sound algorithm with combined sample and deployment guarantees, the novelty beyond Huang et al. (2022) is limited, the required assumptions are strong, and the method appears computationally intractable. Significant revisions are needed to clarify the contributions, justify key assumptions, and address practical implementation concerns.
